735	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
736	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
737	
738	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
739	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
740	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
741	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
742	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
743	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
744	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
745	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
746	
747	\begin{definition}\leanok
748	[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
749	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
750	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
751	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
752	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
753	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
754	  \[
755	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
756	  \]
757	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
758	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
759	\end{definition}
760	
761	\begin{lemma}\leanok
762	[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
763	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
764	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
765	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
766	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
767	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
768	\end{lemma}
769	\begin{proof}
770	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
771	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
772	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\).
773	\end{proof}
774	
775	\begin{definition}\leanok
776	[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
777	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
778	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
779	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
780	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
781	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
782	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
783	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
784	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
785	\end{definition}
786	
787	\begin{lemma}\leanok
788	[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
789	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
790	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
791	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
792	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
793	\end{lemma}
794	\begin{proof}
795	  The list of values of \(v\) consists exactly of the entries \(v_i\); each satisfies
796	  \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\) by hypothesis.
797	\end{proof}
798	
799	\begin{lemma}\leanok
800	[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
801	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
802	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
803	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
804	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
805	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
806	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
807	\end{lemma}
808	\begin{proof}
809	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
810	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
811	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
812	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
813	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
814	\end{proof}
815	
816	\begin{lemma}\leanok
817	[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
818	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
819	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
820	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
821	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
822	  transformed monomial is the radix value
823	  \[
824	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
825	  \]
826	\end{lemma}
827	\begin{proof}
828	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
829	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
830	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
831	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
832	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
833	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
834	\end{proof}
835	
836	\begin{lemma}\leanok
837	[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
838	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
839	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
840	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
841	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
842	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
843	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
844	\end{lemma}
845	\begin{proof}
846	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
847	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
848	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
849	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
850	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
851	\end{proof}
852	
853	\begin{lemma}\leanok
854	[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
855	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
856	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
857	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
858	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
859	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
860	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
861	\end{lemma}
862	\begin{proof}
863	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
864	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
865	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
866	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
867	\end{proof}
868	
869	\begin{lemma}\leanok
870	[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
871	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
872	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
873	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
874	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
875	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
876	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
877	\end{lemma}
878	\begin{proof}
879	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
880	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
881	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
882	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of
883	  \(T(f) = \sum_w T(c_w X^w)\) is then that of \(T(c_v X^v)\), whose leading coefficient is
884	  the constant \(c_v\) by \cref{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}. Since
885	  \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\), \(c_v \ne 0\).
886	\end{proof}
887	
888	\begin{lemma}\leanok
889	[Naturality of the variable-splitting isomorphism under base change]
890	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
891	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
892	  For any ring homomorphism \(\varphi : A \to B\) and polynomial \(q \in A[X_0, \dots, X_m]\),
893	  the isomorphism \(A[X_0, \dots, X_m] \cong (A[X_1, \dots, X_m])[X_0]\) is natural with
894	  respect to applying \(\varphi\) coefficientwise.
895	\end{lemma}

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}
104	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{
109	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
111	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
112	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
113	  %  }
114	  %  \(\)
115	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
116	  %  \end{enumerate}
117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
118	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
133	  \end{enumerate}
134	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
135	  over \(X\).
136	\end{theorem}
137	
138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
139	% Lemmas REF,
140	% REF, and
141	% REF.
142	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
143	% Lemma REF."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
149	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
150	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
151	  open-immersion transition data
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
153	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
154	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
155	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
156	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
157	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
158	\end{proof}

398	\section{Lean encoding}
399	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
400	
401	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
402	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
403	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
404	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
405	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
406	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
407	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
408	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
409	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).
410	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
411	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
412	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
413	(\cref{thm:relative_spec_univ}) is expressed as a
414	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
415	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
416	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
417	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a
418	definitional/structural construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme
419	infrastructure.
420

422	\label{sec:relspec_out_of_scope}
423	
424	This chapter packages \emph{only} the relative-spectrum construction and its core
425	properties. The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling chapters and
426	are explicitly out of scope here:

433	  \item \textbf{Connectedness, dimensionality, smoothness of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)}

436	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety structure} (A.3) --- a

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}
80	
81	\begin{lemma}
82	[Left Cramer inverse]
83	  \label{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul}
84	  \lean{Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul}
85	  \mathlibok
86	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
87	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
88	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A^{-1} A = 1\).
89	\end{lemma}
90	
91	\begin{lemma}
92	[Right Cramer inverse]
93	  \label{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
94	  \lean{Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv}
95	  \mathlibok
96	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
97	  For a square matrix \(A\) over a commutative ring with \(\det A\) a unit, the
98	  nonsingular inverse \(A^{-1}\) satisfies \(A A^{-1} = 1\).
99	\end{lemma}
100	
101	\begin{lemma}
102	[Universal property of an away-localisation]
103	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_lift}
104	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.lift}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
107	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S = R[1/x]\) a localisation
108	  of \(R\) away from \(x\). Given any ring homomorphism \(g : R \to P\) that
109	  sends \(x\) to a unit of \(P\), there is a unique ring homomorphism
110	  \(S \to P\) extending \(g\) along \(R \to S\).
111	\end{lemma}
112	
113	\subsection*{The transition map by matrix algebra}
114	
115	We now build \(\theta_{I,J}\) in seven steps over the localised chart ring
116	\(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), splitting at the matrix-algebra seams. The
117	ambient chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is the polynomial ring of
118	\cref{def:gr_affine_chart} in the \(d(r-d)\) free variables \(x^I_{p,q}\)
119	(\(p \in \{1,\dots,d\}\), \(q \notin I\)).
120	
121	\begin{definition}
122	
123	\leanok
124	[The universal matrix \(X^I\)]
125	  \label{def:gr_universal_matrix}
126	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix}
127	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
128	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
129	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
130	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
131	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
132	  % "For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
133	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
134	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
135	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$."
136	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
137	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). The \emph{universal
138	  matrix} \(X^I\) is the \(d \times r\) matrix over the chart ring
139	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) whose \(I\)-minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\) identity
140	  \(1_{d\times d}\) and whose remaining entries are the free indeterminates
141	  \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\), \(q \notin I\)). It is the universal point of
142	  the chart \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}): a ring map
143	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to A\) is the same as a \(d \times r\) matrix over \(A\)
144	  with identity \(I\)-minor.
145	\end{definition}
146	
147	\begin{definition}
148	
149	\leanok
150	[The minor determinant \(P^I_J\)]
151	  \label{def:gr_minor_det}
152	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.minorDet}
153	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
154	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
155	  % affine patches" (read from
156	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
157	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
158	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
159	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
160	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$."
161	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
162	  For \(J \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(J) = d\), let \(X^I_J\) denote the
163	  \(J\)-th minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix})
164	  -- the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose columns are those indexed by \(J\). Set
165	  \[
166	    P^I_J \;:=\; \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
167	  \]
168	  Then \(R^I_J := \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) (the localisation of the chart ring
169	  away from \(P^I_J\)) is the ring of the principal open subscheme
170	  \(U^I_J = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J] \subseteq U^I\) on which \(P^I_J\) is
171	  invertible. One has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\), so \(R^I_I =
172	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) and \(U^I_I = U^I\).
173	\end{definition}
174	
175	\begin{definition}
176	
177	\leanok
178	[The localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\) over \(R^I_J\)]
179	  \label{def:gr_universal_minor}
180	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinor}
181	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_minor_det}
182	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
183	  % affine patches" (read from
184	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
185	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
186	  % "Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
187	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible."
188	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
189	  Write \(X^I_J \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) for the \(J\)-minor of
190	  the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) with its entries
191	  pushed forward along the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\) into the
192	  localised chart ring \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\)
193	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}). By construction \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of
194	  \(P^I_J\) in \(R^I_J\).
195	\end{definition}
196	
197	\begin{lemma}
198	
199	\leanok
200	[The localised minor determinant is a unit]
201	  \label{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_det_universalMinor}
203	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
204	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
205	  % affine patches" (read from
206	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
209	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
210	  %  on $U^I_J$."
211	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
212	  The determinant of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
213	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}) is a unit of \(R^I_J\):
214	  \[
215	    \det\bigl(X^I_J\bigr) \in (R^I_J)^{\times}.
216	  \]
217	\end{lemma}
218	\begin{proof}
219	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor, def:gr_minor_det, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
220	  \leanok
221	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_minor}, \(\det(X^I_J)\) is the image of \(P^I_J\)
222	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) under the structure map
223	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to R^I_J\). Since \(R^I_J\) is the localisation away from
224	  \(P^I_J\), that image is a unit by
225	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}.
226	\end{proof}
227	
228	\begin{definition}
229	
230	\leanok
231	[The Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\)]
232	  \label{def:gr_universalMinorInv}
233	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv}
234	  \uses{def:gr_universal_minor}
235	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
236	  % affine patches" (read from
237	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
238	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
239	  % "the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
240	  %  on $U^I_J$."
241	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
242	  Let \((X^I_J)^{-1} \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times d}(R^I_J)\) be the
243	  nonsingular (Cramer) inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor \(X^I_J\)
244	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_minor}). Its entries lie in \(R^I_J\) by Cramer's rule.
245	\end{definition}
246	
247	\begin{lemma}
248	
249	\leanok
250	[The Cramer inverse is a two-sided inverse]
251	  \label{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMinorInv_mul_cancel}
253	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
254	  Because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\) (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}),
255	  the Cramer inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) is a
256	  genuine two-sided inverse:
257	  \[
258	    (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}
259	    \qquad\text{and}\qquad
260	    X^I_J\, (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}.
261	  \]
262	\end{lemma}
263	\begin{proof}
264	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul, lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
265	  \leanok
266	  Immediate from \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit} together with
267	  \cref{lem:mathlib_nonsing_inv_mul} (left identity) and
268	  \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv} (right identity), both of which apply once
269	  \(\det(X^I_J)\) is known to be a unit.
270	\end{proof}
271	
272	\begin{definition}
273	
274	\leanok
275	[The image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\)]
276	  \label{def:gr_image_matrix}
277	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix}
278	  \uses{def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_universal_matrix}
279	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
280	  % affine patches" (read from
281	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
282	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
283	  % "The images of the variables
284	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
285	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$."
286	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
287	  Define the \(d \times r\) matrix over \(R^I_J\)
288	  \[
289	    M \;:=\; (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I \;\in\; \operatorname{Mat}_{d \times r}(R^I_J),
290	  \]
291	  the product of the Cramer inverse (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}) with the
292	  universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) base-changed to
293	  \(R^I_J\). Its entries are the prospective images of the indeterminates
294	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) under \(\theta_{I,J}\). Its \(J\)-minor is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J =
295	  1_{d\times d}\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}), so \(M\) is a
296	  point of the chart \(U^J\); and its \(I\)-minor is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I =
297	  (X^I_J)^{-1}\).
298	\end{definition}
299	
300	\begin{definition}
301	
302	\leanok
303	[The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\)]
304	  \label{def:gr_transition_pre}
305	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap}
306	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix}
307	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
308	  % affine patches" (read from
309	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
310	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
311	  % "a ring homomorphism
312	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
313	  %  defined as follows. The images of the variables
314	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
315	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$."
316	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
317	  Let \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\) be the
318	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism out of the chart ring of \(J\) determined,
319	  via the universal property of the polynomial ring, by sending each free
320	  indeterminate \(x^J_{p,q}\) to the \((p,q)\)-entry of the image matrix \(M =
321	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}); equivalently
322	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) as a matrix identity (the
323	  identity \(J\)-block of \(X^J\) matching the identity \(J\)-minor of \(M\)).
324	\end{definition}
325	
326	\begin{lemma}
327	
328	\leanok
329	[The pre-hom sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit]
330	  \label{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}
331	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
332	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
333	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
334	  % affine patches" (read from
335	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
336	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
337	  % "In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
338	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
339	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
340	  The pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre})
341	  sends \(P^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}, the \(I\)-minor determinant of
342	  \(X^J\)) to a unit of \(R^I_J\). Indeed
343	  \[
344	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr)
345	      = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1} = 1/P^I_J,
346	  \]
347	  which is a unit.
348	\end{lemma}
349	\begin{proof}
350	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_minorDet_unit}
351	  \leanok
352	  By \cref{def:gr_transition_pre}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = M = (X^I_J)^{-1}
353	  X^I\), so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) carries the \(I\)-minor \(X^J_I\) of \(X^J\) to
354	  the \(I\)-minor of \(M\), which is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) since
355	  the \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity. Hence \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) =
356	  \det((X^I_J)^{-1}) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\). As \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit
357	  (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), so is its inverse; the matrix-inverse
358	  determinant identity uses \cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}.
359	\end{proof}
360	
361	\begin{definition}
362	
363	\leanok
364	[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
365	  \label{def:gr_transition}
366	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
367	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_affine_chart}
368	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
369	  % affine patches" (read from
370	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
371	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
372	  % "For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
373	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
374	  %  defined as follows. ...
375	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
376	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
377	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
378	  Because the pre-localisation hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to
379	  R^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) sends \(P^J_I\) to a unit of \(R^I_J\)
380	  (\cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}), the universal property of the
381	  away-localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) provides a unique ring
382	  homomorphism extending it along \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\).
383	  This extension is the \emph{transition map}
384	  \[
385	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
386	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J], \qquad \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I .
387	  \]
388	  Dually \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
389	  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), which we also denote
390	  \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the universal matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\).
391	\end{definition}
392	
393	\begin{lemma}
394	
395	\leanok
396	[\(\theta_{I,I}\) is the identity]
397	  \label{lem:gr_transition_self}
398	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap_self}
399	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
400	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
401	  % affine patches" (read from
402	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
403	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
404	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$".
405	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
406	  For every \(I\), \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\): since \(P^I_I = 1\)
407	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) the minor \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\) is its own
408	  inverse, so \(\theta_{I,I}(X^I) = (X^I_I)^{-1} X^I = X^I\), i.e.\
409	  \(\theta_{I,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity ring homomorphism
410	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_I] = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\).
411	\end{lemma}
412	\begin{proof}
413	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_minor_det}
414	  \leanok
415	  When \(J = I\) one has \(P^I_I = \det(1_{d\times d}) = 1\)
416	  (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}), so \(R^I_I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the minor \(X^I_I\)
417	  is the identity, and its Cramer inverse is again the identity. Hence the image
418	  matrix is \(M = 1 \cdot X^I = X^I\), the pre-hom sends each \(x^I_{p,q}\) to
419	  itself, and its localisation lift (\cref{def:gr_transition}) is the identity.
420	\end{proof}
421	
422	\subsection*{Project-local matrix and minor bookkeeping}
423	
424	The cocycle computation rests on a handful of elementary matrix-algebra
425	identities over the (localised) chart rings. They are project-internal helpers
426	with no external source; each is verified directly in Lean.
427	
428	\begin{lemma}
429	
430	\leanok
431	[Column submatrix of a product]
432	  \label{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}
433	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.mul_submatrix_col}
434	  Let \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
435	  \(B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(R)\) be matrices over a commutative ring,
436	  and \(g : \{1,\dots,d\} \to \{1,\dots,r\}\) a column-index map. Then taking the
437	  column submatrix of the product equals multiplying by the column submatrix of
438	  the right factor:
439	  \[
440	    (A B)_{\bullet\, g} \;=\; A \,\bigl(B_{\bullet\, g}\bigr).
441	  \]
442	\end{lemma}
443	\begin{proof}
444	
445	\leanok
446	  Entrywise from the definition of matrix multiplication (reindexing the rows by
447	  the identity leaves the contraction index untouched). Proved directly in Lean.
448	\end{proof}
449	
450	\begin{lemma}
451	
452	\leanok
453	[A ring map commutes with the Cramer inverse]
454	  \label{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}
455	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_nonsing_inv}
456	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
457	  Let \(f : R \to S\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and
458	  \(A \in \operatorname{Mat}_{n\times n}(R)\) a square matrix with \(\det A\) a
459	  unit. Writing \(f_\ast\) for the entrywise application of \(f\), the
460	  nonsingular inverse is natural:
461	  \[
462	    \bigl(f_\ast A\bigr)^{-1} \;=\; f_\ast\bigl(A^{-1}\bigr).
463	  \]
464	\end{lemma}
465	\begin{proof}
466	
467	\leanok
468	  Since \(f_\ast A \cdot f_\ast(A^{-1}) = f_\ast(A A^{-1}) = f_\ast(1_{n\times n})
469	  = 1_{n\times n}\) (using \(A A^{-1} = 1\), \cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}),
470	  the matrix \(f_\ast(A^{-1})\) is a right inverse of \(f_\ast A\), hence equals
471	  \((f_\ast A)^{-1}\). Proved directly in Lean.
472	\end{proof}
473	
474	\begin{lemma}
475	
476	\leanok
477	[Functoriality of entrywise application]
478	  \label{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}
479	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.map_map_eq_of_comp}
480	  Let \(f : R \to A\), \(g : A \to D\) and \(h : R \to D\) be ring
481	  homomorphisms with \(g \circ f = h\). Then for any matrix \(M\) over \(R\),
482	  applying \(f\) then \(g\) entrywise equals applying \(h\) entrywise:
483	  \(g_\ast(f_\ast M) = h_\ast M\).
484	\end{lemma}
485	\begin{proof}
486	
487	\leanok
488	  Immediate from functoriality of \(\operatorname{Mat}(-)\) (composition of
489	  entrywise maps). Proved directly in Lean.
490	\end{proof}

492	\begin{lemma}
493	
494	\leanok
495	[Inverse cancellation in a composite]
496	  \label{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
497	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}
498	  \uses{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}
499	  Let \(A, B \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(R)\) and
500	  \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times e}(R)\) over a commutative ring, with
501	  \(\det A\) a unit. Then
502	  \[
503	    \bigl(B^{-1} A\bigr)\,\bigl(A^{-1} M\bigr) \;=\; B^{-1} M.
504	  \]
505	\end{lemma}
506	\begin{proof}
507	
508	\leanok
509	  By associativity and \(A A^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\)
510	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_mul_nonsing_inv}). Proved directly in Lean.
511	\end{proof}

513	\begin{lemma}
514	
515	\leanok
516	[The \(I\)-minor of \(X^I\) is the identity]
517	  \label{lem:gr_universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
518	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalMatrix_submatrix_self}
519	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix}
520	  The \(I\)-minor of the universal matrix \(X^I\)
521	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), with its \(I\)-columns read through the order
522	  isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\), is the \(d\times d\)
523	  identity: \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\). (This is the normalisation \(P^I_I = 1\)
524	  defining the chart.)
525	\end{lemma}
526	\begin{proof}
527	
528	\leanok
529	  By \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix} the \(q\)-column with \(q \in I\) is the
530	  standard basis vector \(e_p\) where \(p\) is the preimage of \(q\) under the
531	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \xrightarrow{\sim} I\); so the \(I\)-minor
532	  is the identity. Proved directly in Lean.
533	\end{proof}
534	
535	\begin{lemma}
536	
537	\leanok
538	[The \(J\)-minor of the image matrix is the identity]
539	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}
540	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_submatrix_self}

543	  (\cref{def:gr_image_matrix}) is the identity:
544	  \[

546	  \]
547	\end{lemma}
548	\begin{proof}
549	
550	\leanok
551	  The \(J\)-column submatrix of \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is
552	  \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) times the \(J\)-column submatrix of \(X^I\), i.e.
553	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J\), by \cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}; this is
554	  \(1_{d\times d}\) by the left-inverse identity
555	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}). Proved directly in Lean.
556	\end{proof}

585	  \uses{def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_transition_pre, lem:gr_imageMatrix_submatrix_self}

594	
595	\leanok

598	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}). On a column \(q \in J\) the entry of \(X^J\) is

610	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap}
611	  For a third size-\(d\) subset \(K\), the pre-hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) sends the
612	  minor determinant \(P^J_K\) (\cref{def:gr_minor_det}) to the determinant of the
613	  \(K\)-minor of the image matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\):
614	  \[
615	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}\bigl(P^J_K\bigr) \;=\; \det\bigl(M_K\bigr).
616	  \]
617	\end{lemma}

620	\leanok
621	  The hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) commutes with determinants and with passing to
622	  a column submatrix, so \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K) =
623	  \det\bigl((\tilde\theta_{I,J})_\ast X^J\bigr)_K\); apply

627	\begin{lemma}
628	
629	\leanok
630	[Naturality of the image matrix]
631	  \label{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}
632	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.imageMatrix_map_eq}
633	  \uses{def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_universal_minor}
634	  Let \(X\) be a size-\(d\) subset and \(\iota : R^I_X \to D\) a ring
635	  homomorphism lying over the structure map \(R^I \to D\), i.e.\
636	  \(\iota \circ (R^I \to R^I_X) = (R^I \to D)\). Writing \(Y\) for the universal
637	  matrix \(X^I\) base-changed to \(D\) and \(Y_X\) for its \(X\)-minor, one has
638	  \[
639	    \iota_\ast\bigl((X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\bigr) \;=\; (Y_X)^{-1}\, Y .
640	  \]
641	\end{lemma}
642	\begin{proof}
643	
644	\leanok
645	  Distribute \(\iota_\ast\) across the product \(M = (X^I_X)^{-1} X^I\); the
646	  factor \((X^I_X)^{-1}\) passes through \(\iota_\ast\) as a Cramer inverse
647	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv}, using \cref{lem:gr_minorDet_unit}), and both
648	  factors collapse to base changes over \(D\) by functoriality
649	  (\cref{lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp}) since \(\iota\) lies over \(R^I \to D\).
650	  Proved directly in Lean.
651	\end{proof}

653	\subsection*{Triple-overlap rings and the localised transition maps}
654	
655	The cocycle condition \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) cannot
656	be stated as a naive composition of the landed transition maps of
657	\cref{def:gr_transition}: the codomain of \(\theta_{J,K}\) (the ring
658	\(R^J[1/P^J_K]\)) differs from the domain of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (the ring
659	\(R^J[1/P^J_I]\)). The identity therefore lives over the \emph{triple-overlap}
660	rings obtained by inverting both relevant minors in each chart ring:
661	\[
662	  S_K := R^K[1/(P^K_I P^K_J)], \quad
663	  S_J := R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)], \quad
664	  S_I := R^I[1/(P^I_J P^I_K)].
665	\]
666	
667	\begin{definition}
668	
669	\leanok
670	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (left)]
671	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_left}
672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft}
673	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
674	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
675	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} : R[1/x] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
676	  \(y\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
677	  the structure map \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(x\) — well-defined because \(x\)
678	  is a unit of \(R[1/(xy)]\) (a factor of the unit \(xy\),
679	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
680	\end{definition}
681	
682	\begin{definition}
683	
684	\leanok
685	[Inclusion of an away-localisation into a double one (right)]
686	  \label{def:gr_away_incl_right}
687	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclRight}
688	  \uses{lem:mathlib_away_lift, lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
689	  For \(x, y \in R\), the canonical inclusion
690	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{x,y} : R[1/y] \to R[1/(xy)]\) inverting the extra factor
691	  \(x\), defined as the away-localisation lift (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) of
692	  \(R \to R[1/(xy)]\) along \(y\) — well-defined since \(y\) is a unit of
693	  \(R[1/(xy)]\) (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}).
694	\end{definition}
695	
696	\begin{lemma}
697	
698	\leanok
699	[Left inclusion lies over \(R\)]
700	  \label{lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}
701	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap}
702	  \uses{def:gr_away_incl_left}

704	  intertwines the two structure maps:
705	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{x,y} \circ (R \to R[1/x]) = (R \to R[1/(xy)])\).
706	\end{lemma}
707	\begin{proof}
708	
709	\leanok
710	  By the defining property of the away-localisation lift
711	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}). Proved directly in Lean.
712	\end{proof}

766	\begin{lemma}
767	
768	\leanok
769	[The cross minor becomes a unit after inclusion]
770	  \label{lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
771	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
772	  \uses{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, def:gr_image_matrix}
773	  Let \(A, B, C\) be size-\(d\) subsets and \(\iota : R^A_B \to D\) a ring
774	  homomorphism over the structure map \(R^A \to D\) under which the minor
775	  determinant \(P^A_C\) becomes a unit of \(D\). Then
776	  \(\iota\bigl(\tilde\theta_{A,B}(P^B_C)\bigr)\) is a unit of \(D\). Indeed
777	  \[
778	    \tilde\theta_{A,B}\bigl(P^B_C\bigr)
779	      = \det\bigl((X^A_B)^{-1} X^A_C\bigr)
780	      = \det\bigl((X^A_B)^{-1}\bigr)\cdot P^A_C ,
781	  \]

789	  the \(C\)-minor of the product (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}) and the
790	  determinant as \(\det((X^A_B)^{-1}) \cdot P^A_C\). The first factor is a unit
791	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}); the second becomes a unit under
792	  \(\iota\) by hypothesis. Proved directly in Lean.
793	\end{proof}

795	\begin{definition}

849	\begin{lemma}
850	
851	\leanok
852	[Image-matrix identity over the triple overlap]
853	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
854	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
855	  \uses{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_away_incl_left, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayInclLeft_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap, lem:gr_map_map_eq_of_comp, lem:gr_map_nonsing_inv, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel, lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left, lem:gr_minorDet_unit, def:gr_image_matrix, def:gr_universal_minor}
856	  Over the triple-overlap ring \(S_I\), the image matrix of \(\theta_{I,K}\)
857	  equals \(\Theta_{I,J}\) applied entrywise to the image matrix of
858	  \(\theta_{J,K}\):
859	  \[
860	    \iota^{\mathrm{R}}_\ast\bigl((X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\bigr)
861	      \;=\;
862	      \bigl(\Theta_{I,J}\circ\iota^{\mathrm{R}}\bigr)_\ast
863	        \bigl((X^J_K)^{-1} X^J\bigr) .
864	  \]
865	  Both sides reduce to \((Y_K)^{-1} Y\), where \(Y\) is the universal matrix
866	  \(X^I\) base-changed to \(S_I\) and \(Y_K\) its \(K\)-minor.
867	\end{lemma}
868	\begin{proof}
869	
870	\leanok
871	  The left side is \((Y_K)^{-1} Y\) by naturality of the image matrix
872	  (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}, via \cref{lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap}).
873	  For the right side, \(\Theta_{I,J}\circ\iota^{\mathrm{R}}\) carries the chart
874	  ring \(R^J\) over \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}}\circ\tilde\theta_{I,J}\), so the image
875	  matrix of \(\theta_{J,K}\) maps to \((M^J_K)^{-1} M^J\) with
876	  \(M^J = (Y_J)^{-1} Y\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap},
877	  \cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_eq}); the minor \(M^J_K = (Y_J)^{-1} Y_K\)
878	  (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}) inverts via \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1}A^{-1}\), and
879	  the cancellation \((Y_K)^{-1} Y_J \cdot (Y_J)^{-1} Y = (Y_K)^{-1} Y\)
880	  (\cref{lem:gr_inv_mul_inv_mul_cancel}, using \(\det Y_J\) a unit by
881	  \cref{lem:gr_isUnit_algebraMap_away_left}) gives \((Y_K)^{-1} Y\). Proved
882	  directly in Lean.
883	\end{proof}
884	
885	\begin{lemma}
886	
887	\leanok
888	[Cocycle condition for the transition maps]
889	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle}
890	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleCondition}
891	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
892	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
893	  % affine patches" (read from
894	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
895	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
896	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
897	  %  we leave it to the reader to verify that
898	  %  for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
899	  %  cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
900	  %  $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
901	  %  is satisfied."
902	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
903	  For any three subsets \(I, J, K \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) of cardinality
904	  \(d\), the transition maps of \cref{def:gr_transition} satisfy the cocycle
905	  condition. As morphisms of schemes, on the triple overlap inside \(U^I\),
906	  \[
907	    \theta_{I,K} \;=\; \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J},
908	  \]
909	  equivalently, as ring homomorphisms,
910	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) (the two read the same
911	  identity through the order-reversal of \(\Spec\)). Together with
912	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\), this is exactly the compatibility datum required to
913	  glue the charts.
914	\end{lemma}
915	
916	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
917	% we leave it to the reader to verify that
918	% for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
919	% cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
920	% $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
921	% is satisfied."  (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
922	% L838-L848.)
923	\begin{proof}
924	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_image_matrix, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities, lem:gr_transition_self, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ik, lem:gr_cocycle_imageMatrix_eq}
925	  \leanok
926	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
927	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
928	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
929	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
930	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
931	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
932	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).
933	
934	  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
935	  \cref{def:gr_transition},
936	  \[
937	    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
938	  \]
939	  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
940	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
941	  \[
942	    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
943	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
944	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
945	  \]
946	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
947	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
948	  \[
949	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
950	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
951	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
952	  \]
953	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
954	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
955	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
956	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
957	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
958	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
959	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
960	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
961	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
962	\end{proof}

964	\section{Scheme-level glue-data layer}
965	\label{sec:gr_glue_layer}
966	
967	The transition data of \cref{sec:gr_transition} lives at the level of rings. To
968	feed it to a scheme-gluing machine we must re-express each piece as a morphism in
969	the category of schemes: the charts \(U^I\), the principal-open overlaps
970	\(U^I_J\), their inclusions, and the transitions \(\theta_{I,J}\) all become
971	schemes and scheme morphisms by applying the contravariant functor \(\Spec\).
972	The lemmas of this section record those translations together with the two
973	genuinely geometric facts needed to assemble a glue datum: that each inclusion
974	\(U^I_J \hookrightarrow U^I\) is an open immersion, and that the fibre product of
975	two principal opens of \(\Spec A\) is again a principal open. They are
976	project-internal bridge results with no external source beyond the gluing
977	construction of \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}.
978

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).

54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on

79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}

140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
160	  % Mathlib at the pinned commit. The degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
161	  % requires a tensor power \(L_s^{\otimes m}\) of a line bundle; this infrastructure (tensor
162	  % product of sheaves of modules with lax-monoidal global sections) is not yet available, making
163	  % this definition a prerequisite sub-build.
164	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
165	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
166	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
169	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
170	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
171	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
172	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
173	  %  the function
174	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
175	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
176	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
177	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
178	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
179	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
180	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
181	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
182	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
183	  \[
184	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
185	  \]
186	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
187	  the tensor product of sections
188	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
189	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
190	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
191	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
192	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
193	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
194	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
195	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
196	  polynomial ring).
197	\end{definition}
