212	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
213	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
214	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
215	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
216	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
217	  I.7.}
218	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
219	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
220	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
221	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
222	  \[
223	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
224	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
225	  \]
226	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
227	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
228	  by the section tensor product
229	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
230	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
231	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
232	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
233	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
234	\end{definition}
235	
236	\begin{lemma}
237	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
238	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
241	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
242	  % Polynomials" (read from
243	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
245	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
246	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
247	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
248	  %  the function
249	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
250	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
251	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
252	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
253	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
254	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
255	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
256	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
257	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
258	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
259	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
260	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
261	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
262	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
267	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
268	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
269	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
270	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
271	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
272	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
273	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
274	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
275	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
276	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
277	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
278	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
279	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
280	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
281	\end{proof}
282	
283	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
284	  graded series]
285	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
286	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
287	  \mathlibok
288	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
289	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
290	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
291	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
292	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
293	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
294	  equals \(h(n)\):
295	  \[
296	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
297	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
298	  \]
299	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
300	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
301	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
302	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
303	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
304	\end{lemma}
305	
306	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
307	  (rank--nullity)]
308	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
309	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
310	  \mathlibok
311	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
312	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
313	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
314	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
315	  \[
316	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
317	  \]
318	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
319	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
320	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
321	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
322	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
323	\end{lemma}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}[The rational power series \((1 - X)^{-d}\)]
326	  \label{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
327	  \lean{PowerSeries.invOneSubPow}
328	  \mathlibok
329	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
330	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PowerSeries.WellKnown}).}
331	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then \((1 - X)^d\) is a
332	  unit in the power series ring \(S\llbracket X\rrbracket\), and Mathlib names its
333	  inverse
334	  \[
335	    \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(S, d) \;=\; (1 - X)^{-d}
336	      \;\in\; S\llbracket X\rrbracket^{\times}.
337	  \]
338	  Together with the coercion
339	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries} : F[X] \to F\llbracket X\rrbracket\) of a
340	  polynomial to its power series, this is the data in which a rational Hilbert
341	  series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) is expressed; it is the same rational-series shape
342	  whose eventual coefficients are extracted by
343	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
344	\end{lemma}
345	
346	\begin{lemma}\leanok
347	[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
348	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
350	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
351	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
352	    lem:ratHilb_bump, def:graded_subquotientHilb,
353	    lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
354	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
355	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
356	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
357	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
358	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
359	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
360	  %  function
361	  %  $$
362	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
363	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
364	  %  $$
365	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
366	  %  If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
367	  %  then this function is a numerical polynomial."
368	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
369	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
370	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
371	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
372	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
373	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
374	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
375	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
376	  such that for all \(n > N\)
377	  \[
378	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
379	  \]
380	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
381	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
382	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
383	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
384	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
385	
386	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
387	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
388	  module). It is not covered by Mathlib: \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
389	  supplies only the converse polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not
390	  the production of the rational series from the module. The argument is realised by
391	  an \emph{ambient subquotient induction} (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}):
392	  the whole induction ranges over pairs of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N\) of the
393	  single fixed graded module \(M\), and the Hilbert function is the ambient difference
394	  \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such
395	  pairs is closed under the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
396	  element (each presented again as an ambient subquotient pair), so the inductive step
397	  never forms a graded quotient ring \(R/(x)\), a graded quotient module \(M/xM\), or a
398	  regrading of a derived carrier. The genuine Mathlib gap is thereby \emph{avoided}
399	  rather than filled; the residual obligation is the short ambient bookkeeping of the
400	  subquotient blocks (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb},
401	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker},
402	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff},
403	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer},
404	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}), each of which manipulates only ambient
405	  families \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\) of submodules of \(M\).
406	  % NOTE: The power-series rationality toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}) is formalized
407	  %   and axiom-clean; the present theorem is a closed, axiom-clean Lean declaration
408	  %   (gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational). The ambient subquotient route
409	  %   (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}) sidesteps missing graded-quotient API by
410	  %   working with ambient families of submodules of \(M\).
411	\end{lemma}
412	
413	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
414	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
415	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
416	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
417	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
418	%  following short exact sequence
419	%  $$
420	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
421	%  $$
422	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
423	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
424	\begin{proof}
425	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
426	    lem:ratHilb_bump, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
427	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
428	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
429	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb,
430	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
431	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
432	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib,
433	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
434	    \leanok
435	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
436	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
437	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
438	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
439	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
440	
441	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
442	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
443	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
444	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
445	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
446	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). The degree-one part
447	  \(R_1\) is then a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space whose \(\kappa\)-basis
448	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) generates \(R\) as a \(\kappa\)-algebra; \(r = \dim_\kappa R_1\)
449	  is the number on which we induct.
450	  % NOTE: The existence of finitely many degree-one generators (the number r the
451	  % induction is on) is expected to follow from the standing hypotheses --
452	  % "finitely generated κ-algebra" together with "generated in degree 1" -- so no
453	  % new hypothesis on gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational is anticipated. To be
454	  % confirmed when the prover formalizes the assembly: degree-1 generation gives
455	  % R₁ as a finite-dimensional κ-vector space spanning R as a κ-algebra, and a
456	  % κ-basis of R₁ is a finite degree-one generating set.
457	
458	  \emph{Set-up of the ambient data.} Regard \(M\) as the fixed ambient graded
459	  \(\kappa\)-module with internal decomposition \(n \mapsto \mathcal{M}_n := M_n\)
460	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}); the components are finite-dimensional. For each
461	  basis vector \(x_i \in R_1\) let \(t_i : M \to M\) be multiplication by \(x_i\), a
462	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism that raises degree by one (it sends \(M_n\) into
463	  \(M_{n+1}\), \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}). Since \(R\) is
464	  commutative the \(t_i\) pairwise commute, and they trivially preserve the pair of
465	  homogeneous submodules \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\). Because \(R\) is generated by
466	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) and \(M\) is finitely generated over \(R\), \(M\) is finitely
467	  generated as a module over the polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) acting
468	  through the \(t_i\); this is the finiteness condition required below.
469	
470	  \emph{Invoking the ambient subquotient induction.} Apply
471	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} to the pair \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\)
472	  with the \(r\) commuting degree-\((+1)\) endomorphisms \(t_1, \dots, t_r\). It
473	  yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) of order \(r\) for the ambient subquotient Hilbert
474	  function of this pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}),
475	  \[
476	    h(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(\top \cap \mathcal{M}_n) - \dim_\kappa(\bot \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
477	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n - 0 \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n .
478	  \]
479	  Hence \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa M_n,\, r)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}):
480	  there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d := r \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
481	  with
482	  \[
483	    \dim_\kappa M_n \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)
484	    \qquad (n > N),
485	  \]
486	  which is the claim, with pole order \(d = r\). The internal mechanics of
487	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} -- the base case via
488	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}, and the inductive step assembling the
489	  kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}), the degreewise
490	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, built on
491	  \(D_5\), \cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) and the finiteness transfer
492	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) into
493	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} and \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} -- are the Stacks~00K1
494	  induction realised entirely with ambient homogeneous submodules of \(M\).
495	\end{proof}
496	
497	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
498	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
499	% NOTE: Declarations in this subsection are currently \texttt{private} in the Lean
500	% source; \lean{} pins may not resolve until they are moved to a dedicated module.
501	
502	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
503	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
504	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
505	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
506	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
507	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
508	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
509	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
510	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
511	
512	\begin{lemma}\leanok
513	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
514	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
516	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
517	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
518	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
519	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
520	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
521	  \(F\):
522	  \[
523	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
524	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
525	  \]
526	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
527	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
528	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
529	  the Cauchy product.
530	\end{lemma}
531	
532	\begin{lemma}\leanok
533	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
534	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
535	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
536	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
537	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
538	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
539	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
540	  \[
541	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
542	  \]
543	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
544	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
545	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
546	  \[
547	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
548	  \]
549	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
550	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
551	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
552	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
553	\end{lemma}
554	
555	\begin{proof}
556	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
557	  \leanok
558	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
559	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
560	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
561	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
562	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
563	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
564	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
565	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
566	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
567	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
568	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
569	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
570	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
571	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
572	\end{proof}
573	
574	\begin{definition}\leanok
575	[Rational Hilbert function]
576	  \label{def:ratHilb}
577	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
578	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
579	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
580	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
581	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
582	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
583	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
584	  \[
585	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
586	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
587	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
588	  \]
589	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
590	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
591	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
592	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
593	\end{definition}
594	
595	\begin{lemma}\leanok
596	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
597	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
598	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
599	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
600	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
601	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
602	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
603	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
604	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
605	\end{lemma}
606	
607	\begin{lemma}\leanok
608	[Raising the pole order]
609	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
610	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
611	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
612	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
613	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
614	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
615	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
616	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
617	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
618	\end{lemma}
619	
620	\begin{lemma}\leanok
621	[Closure under difference]
622	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
623	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
624	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
625	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
626	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
627	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
628	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
629	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
630	\end{lemma}
631	
632	\begin{lemma}\leanok
633	[Closure under right shift]
634	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
635	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
636	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
637	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
638	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
639	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
640	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
641	  Lean.
642	\end{lemma}
643	
644	\begin{lemma}\leanok
645	[Antidifference step for the predicate]
646	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
647	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
648	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
649	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
650	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
651	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
652	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
653	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
654	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
655	\end{lemma}
656	
657	\begin{proof}
658	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
659	  \leanok
660	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
661	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
662	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
663	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
664	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
665	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
666	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
667	\end{proof}

669	\begin{lemma}\leanok
670	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
671	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
673	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
674	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
675	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
676	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
677	  \[
678	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
679	  \]
680	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
681	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
682	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
683	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
684	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
685	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
686	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
687	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
688	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
689	\end{lemma}
690	
691	\begin{proof}
692	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
693	  \leanok
694	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
695	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
696	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
697	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
698	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
699	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
700	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).

951	  isomorphisms shows that the base-change map is an isomorphism, as asserted.
952	\end{proof}

954	\subsection{The section-level base-change identity}
955	
956	The section-level identification
957	Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange} below is the single
958	highest-leverage obligation of the affine close. In the affine--affine model the
959	base-change map \(\theta\) is, after its domain and codomain are read through the two
960	proved tilde dictionaries, a concrete \(R'\)-linear map
961	\(R' \otimes_R M \to (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\), and the content of the lemma is
962	that this map is the inverse of the cancellation isomorphism
963	\(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\). Because the base-change map is \emph{defined} as the
964	transpose of the inner pushforward comparison \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) under the
965	\((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-adjunction (\(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\)), its section-level value
966	is read off by the \emph{conjugate-counit calculus}: unfolding the transpose through the
967	counit form of the hom-set adjunction
968	(\(\operatorname{Adjunction.homEquiv\_counit}\)) factors \(\theta\) as
969	\(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\) followed by the geometric counit \(\varepsilon_g\), and
970	conjugating this composite by the two tilde dictionaries
971	\(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}) and
972	\(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}) replaces the
973	geometric counit by the \emph{algebraic} extension-of-scalars counit along \(\psi\). This
974	is the route the live formalization takes: the cancellation identity
975	Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange} routes through the generator
976	trace Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_generator_trace}, which is a one-line corollary of the
977	section identity Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_section_identity}, whose single residual
978	obligation is the counit-conjugate coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}.
979	The per-generator/element evaluation is \emph{not} available as a shortcut: evaluating the
980	opaque geometric composite on \(1 \otimes m\) produces no element-level normal form that
981	\(\operatorname{rfl}\) or \(\operatorname{simp}\) can reduce, so the abstract conjugate
982	calculus is the only tractable discharge. Two Mathlib results are recorded
983	first as dependency anchors.
984	
985	\begin{lemma}[Pullback of a pair of affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-maps]
986	  \label{lem:pullbackSpecIso_mathlib}
987	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackSpecIso}
988	  \mathlibok
989	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
990	  Let \(R \to S\) and \(R \to T\) be \(R\)-algebras. Then the fibre product of the
991	  induced morphisms \(\operatorname{Spec}(S) \to \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) and
992	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(T) \to \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is canonically the spectrum of
993	  the tensor product,
994	  \[
995	    \operatorname{Limits.pullback}\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}(R\to S),\,
996	      \operatorname{Spec}(R\to T)\bigr)
997	    \;\cong\; \operatorname{Spec}\bigl(S \otimes_R T\bigr)
998	    \qquad (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackSpecIso}),
999	  \]
1000	  and under this isomorphism the two cone legs are the \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-maps of
1001	  the canonical tensor inclusions: \texttt{pullbackSpecIso\_hom\_fst} identifies the
1002	  first projection with \(\operatorname{Spec}\) of
1003	  \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.includeLeftRingHom} : S \to S \otimes_R T\), and
1004	  \texttt{pullbackSpecIso\_hom\_snd} identifies the second projection with
1005	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\) of
1006	  \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.includeRight} : T \to S \otimes_R T\) (with companion
1007	  forms \texttt{pullbackSpecIso\_inv\_fst}, \texttt{\_inv\_snd}).
1008	\end{lemma}

1010	\begin{lemma}[Cancellation of base change for the tensor tower]
1011	  \label{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1012	  \lean{TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange}
1013	  \mathlibok
1014	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1015	  Let \(R \to A \to B\) be a tower of algebras, \(M\) a \(B\)-module that is also an
1016	  \(A\)- and \(R\)-module compatibly, and \(N\) an \(R\)-module. Then there is a
1017	  \(B\)-linear equivalence, carrying \emph{no} flatness hypothesis,
1018	  \[
1019	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
1020	    M \otimes_A (A \otimes_R N) \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M \otimes_R N,
1021	  \]
1022	  with \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\bigl(m \otimes_A (a \otimes_R n)\bigr)
1023	  = (a \cdot m) \otimes_R n\) (\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}) and inverse
1024	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}(m \otimes_R n) = m \otimes_A (1 \otimes_R n)\)
1025	  (\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}). Instantiated at the affine--affine model
1026	  (\(M = R'\), \(B = R'\), \(N = M\)) this is the cancellation isomorphism
1027	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \cong R' \otimes_R M\) used below, read in either
1028	  orientation.
1029	\end{lemma}

1032	  \label{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}

1034	  \mathlibok

1037	  \(A\)-algebra and \(S\)-algebra making the square \(R \to S,\ R \to A,\ A \to B,\

1039	  A B]\). Then for any \(A\)-module \(M\) there is a bundled \(S\)-linear equivalence

1199	  % abstract `leftAdjointCompIso`. The `leftAdjointCompIso` (conjugate-native) form is the separate
1200	  % decl base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs_conj (conj-1a), which carries the two Mathlib anchors
1201	  % lem:leftAdjointCompIso_mathlib and lem:conjugateEquiv_leftAdjointCompIso_inv_mathlib.
1202	  It is the variable-legs form of the codomain read
1203	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}. Fix \(\psi : R \to R'\),
1204	  \(\varphi : R \to A\) and an \(A\)-module \(M\); let \(e : X' \xrightarrow{\sim}
1205	  \operatorname{Spec}(A \otimes_R R')\) be the canonical isomorphism and
1206	  \(\iota_A : A \to A \otimes_R R'\), \(\iota_{R'} : R' \to A \otimes_R R'\) the tensor
1207	  inclusions. Reading the codomain \(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\) along the \emph{explicit composite}
1208	  legs \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\) and \(f' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_{R'}\)
1209	  (carried as free variables together with the leg-equality hypotheses
1210	  \(\mathit{hfst}/\mathit{hsnd}\)), there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R'\)-modules
1211	  \[
1212	    \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}^{\flat} :
1213	    \Gamma\bigl(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\bigr)
1214	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1215	    \operatorname{restr}_{\iota_{R'}}\bigl(\operatorname{ext}_{\iota_A} M\bigr)
1216	    \;=\; (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M ,
1217	  \]
1218	  assembled with its pullback factor taken as the project pseudofunctor pullback-composition
1219	  coherence \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A}\) — the square's commutativity
1220	  entering through the pushforward congruence \(\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\) and the pushforward
1221	  composition coherence \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}\) — followed by the affine pullback dictionary
1222	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}) along \(\iota_A\) and the affine pushforward dictionary
1223	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}) along \(\iota_{R'}\). Carrying the legs as free
1224	  variables (rather than the literal projections) is the structural device that lets them be
1225	  eliminated by substitution on a well-typed motive. Instantiating at the
1226	  literal projection legs \(g' = \operatorname{pr}_1\), \(f' = \operatorname{pr}_2\) recovers
1227	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}.
1228	\end{lemma}
1229	
1230	\begin{proof}
1231	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso,
1232	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
1233	  The construction is the verbatim assembly of
1234	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}, with the cone legs presented as free variables
1235	  \(g', f'\) carrying their factorizations \(\mathit{hfst}/\mathit{hsnd}\) through
1236	  \(e\) as explicit hypotheses. The pullback factor is the project pseudofunctor coherence
1237	  \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A}\); composing it with the two affine
1238	  dictionaries (Lemmas~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, \ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso})
1239	  yields \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}^{\flat}\). Its agreement with
1240	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read} at the literal projection legs is the cone-leg
1241	  factorization.

1244	% --- Supporting lemmas for the codomain read ---
1245	% These helpers establish that pullback along a scheme isomorphism is an equivalence
1246	% of module categories; the adjunction unit of an equivalence is an isomorphism,
1247	% used in the codomain-read computation.
1248	
1249	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1250	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1251	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1253	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1254	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1255	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1256	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1257	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1258	\end{lemma}
1259	\begin{proof}
1260	
1261	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1262	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1263	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1264	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1265	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1266	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1267	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1268	\end{proof}
1269	
1270	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1271	[The pullback functor along an isomorphism is an equivalence]
1272	  \label{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1273	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1274	  \uses{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1275	  Consequently, for an isomorphism \(f : X \to Y\) the functor
1276	  \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is an equivalence of
1277	  categories. In particular its adjunction unit is a natural isomorphism, so
1278	  \((\eta_{f})_{W}\) is invertible on every object \(W\).
1279	\end{lemma}
1280	\begin{proof}
1281	
1282	  The functor underlying the equivalence of
1283	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso} is exactly \(f^*\), so \(f^*\) is an
1284	  equivalence; an equivalence's unit is a natural isomorphism.
1285	\end{proof}

1287	% --- Section-level identity, three pieces: regroup equiv (pure tensor algebra, native
1288	%     R'-linear via Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange) ← section identity (Γ(θ) is the
1289	%     R'-base change of the algebraic unit) ← IsIso corollary (the pinned leaf). ---
1290	
1291	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1292	[Regrouping isomorphism for the section-level mate]
1293	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1294	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1295	  % NOTE: tensor-order convention. The Lean uses Mathlib's `pullbackSpecIso`
1296	  % orientation `A ⊗[R] R'` (so the codomain is `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` and the generator
1297	  % goes `r' ⊗ m ↦ (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m`); the prose below writes `R' ⊗_R A`. The two are the
1298	  % same module up to `TensorProduct.comm` — a faithful re-orientation, no content change.
1299	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1300	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1301	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1302	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1303	  % L933--938).
1304	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1305	  % equality
1306	  % $$
1307	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1308	  % $$
1309	  % as $R'$-modules."
1310	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1311	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1312	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\) an
1313	  \(A\)-module. There is a bundled \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
1314	  \[
1315	    \operatorname{regroup} :
1316	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
1317	  \]
1318	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the source, sending the generator
1319	  \((r' \otimes 1) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\); equivalently its inverse sends
1320	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
1321	  hypothesis.
1322	\end{lemma}
1323	
1324	\begin{proof}
1325	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1326	  The underlying \(R'\)-linear equivalence is supplied, already as a bundled
1327	  \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over \(R'\), by the natively \(R'\)-linear
1328	  pushout-cancellation equivalence of
1329	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}. Crucially, that helper bundles
1330	  \(R'\)-linearity \emph{internally}: there is no hand-written scalar-compatibility
1331	  obligation, no induction over elementary tensors, and in particular \emph{no} residual
1332	  \(r' \cdot 0 = 0\) bookkeeping branch. The only work left to this proof is therefore to
1333	  promote that \(R'\)-linear equivalence to a morphism of \(R'\)-module \emph{objects} in
1334	  the exact extension/restriction-of-scalars packaging that the codomain and domain reads
1335	  produce. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1336	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1337	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1338	
1339	  \emph{The lone residual obstruction is the \(A\)-action diamond, resolved at the object
1340	  level.} The pushout-cancellation helper takes its relative tensor product over the
1341	  \emph{canonical} \(A\)-algebra structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1342	  (multiplication in the left factor), whereas the carrier object produced by the
1343	  dictionaries,
1344	  the extension of scalars of \(M\) along the left inclusion \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\),
1345	  tensors over the \(A\)-module structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\) induced by \(\iota\). These
1346	  two \(A\)-module structures on
1347	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) agree on elements but are distinct as data, so the two relative
1348	  tensor products are distinct types even though their underlying abelian groups and
1349	  their \(R'\)-actions coincide. This diamond is the \emph{only} thing the helper does
1350	  not already discharge, and — because the \(R'\)-linearity is now entirely internal to
1351	  the Mathlib equivalence — it is resolved purely at the carrier level by an
1352	  identity-on-elements \(R'\)-linear bridge
1353	  \[
1354	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M
1355	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1356	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M ,
1357	  \]
1358	  the identity map on generators, which is \(R'\)-linear because both sides carry the
1359	  same \(R'\)-action (restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\)).
1360	
1361	  Composing the bridge \(eT\) with the natively \(R'\)-linear helper equivalence and
1362	  promoting to an isomorphism of module objects yields the desired bundled
1363	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) objects. Being a composite
1364	  of linear equivalences it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents
1365	  carries a flatness hypothesis.
1366	\end{proof}

1372	%       (3) lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose — the (g^* ⊣ g_*) transpose on sections.

1374	\begin{lemma}
1375	
1376	\leanok
1377	[The affine pullback--pushforward unit is the algebraic unit]
1378	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}
1379	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_unit_value}
1380	  \uses{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
1381	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma `lemma-affine-base-change'
1382	  % (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the "boils down to the equality" step
1383	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L932--937).
1384	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the equality
1385	  % $$ (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M $$ as $R'$-modules."
1386	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base change''
1387	  (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1388	  Let \(\iota_A : A \to A \otimes_R R'\) be the canonical inclusion
1389	  (\(a \mapsto a \otimes 1\)). The unit of the
1390	  \(\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)^*, (\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_*\bigr)\)-adjunction
1391	  evaluated at the affine sheaf \(\widetilde M\), read on the global sections over
1392	  \(\operatorname{Spec} A\) through the tilde dictionaries
1393	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}) and
1394	  the tilde--\(\Gamma\) unit \(\widetilde{(-)}\Gamma\)-iso, equals the algebraic unit of the
1395	  extension/restriction-of-scalars adjunction along \(\iota_A\), namely
1396	  \(\eta_M : M \to (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\), \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\).
1397	\end{lemma}
1398	\begin{proof}
1399	  \uses{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
1400	  This is the affine, square-free heart of the computation: there is no pullback square and
1401	  no base-change map yet, only the comparison of two units of two adjunctions related by the
1402	  two tilde dictionaries. The geometric adjunction
1403	  \(\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)^* \dashv (\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_*\bigr)\) on
1404	  sheaves of modules transports, under the tilde dictionaries which identify
1405	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)^*\) of a tilde with extension of scalars
1406	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}) and \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_*\) of a tilde
1407	  with restriction of scalars (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}), to the algebraic
1408	  extension/restriction adjunction \((\iota_A^{\,*} \dashv \iota_{A\,*})\) on
1409	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\). The dictionaries being the comparison isomorphisms of these
1410	  adjunctions, naturality identifies the two units; evaluating at \(M\) gives the stated
1411	  value \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\).
1412	\end{proof}

1663	\begin{lemma}

1665	\leanok
1666	[Pseudofunctoriality of the pullback--pushforward unit]
1667	  \label{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1668	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1669	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1670	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1671	  Let \(a : X_1 \to X_2\) and \(b : X_2 \to X_3\) be composable morphisms of schemes and let
1672	  \(N\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_{X_3}\)-module. Write \(\eta^{h}\) for the unit of the
1673	  pullback--pushforward adjunction \((h^{*}\dashv h_{*})\) attached to a morphism \(h\), and
1674	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b}\), \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b}\) for the pseudofunctor
1675	  composition coherences. Then
1676	  \[
1677	    \eta^{b}_{N} \;\circ\; b_{*}\bigl(\eta^{a}_{\,b^{*}N}\bigr)
1678	      \;\circ\; (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{N}
1679	    \;=\;
1680	    \eta^{a\circ b}_{N} \;\circ\;
1681	      (a\circ b)_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}_{N}\bigr),
1682	  \]
1683	  where composites are read left to right. In words: the unit of the composite morphism
1684	  \(a \circ b\), reindexed by the inverse pullback coherence, factors as the \(b\)-unit, followed
1685	  by \(b_{*}\) of the \(a\)-unit, followed by the pushforward coherence. This is the leg-reindex
1686	  engine consumed by Seam~2 (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex}): with \(a\) an
1687	  isomorphism and \(b\) an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map it turns the generic projection-leg
1688	  unit into the affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-unit modulo the transparent coherences.
1689	\end{lemma}

1692	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1693	  Apply the unit-across-conjugate coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib} to the
1694	  composite adjunction \((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}) . (a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\), the adjunction
1695	  \(\bigl((a\circ b)^{*}\dashv (a\circ b)_{*}\bigr)\), and the left-adjoint transformation
1696	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\). This yields an identity whose right-hand factor
1697	  is the conjugate (mate) of \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\); by
1698	  Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib} that mate is exactly the pushforward
1699	  coherence \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}\). Expanding the unit of the composite
1700	  adjunction by Lemma~\ref{lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib} —
1701	  \(\bigl((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}).(a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\bigr)\text{-unit}_N
1702	    = \eta^{b}_N \circ b_{*}(\eta^{a}_{b^{*}N})\) — and reassociating the resulting composite gives
1703	  the stated equation.

703	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
704	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
705	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
706	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
707	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
708	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
709	  % Polynomials" (read from
710	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
711	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
712	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
713	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
714	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
715	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
716	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
717	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
718	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
719	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
720	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
721	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
722	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
723	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
724	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
725	  \[
726	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
727	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
728	  \]
729	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
730	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
731	\end{theorem}
732	
733	\begin{proof}
734	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
735	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
736	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
737	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
738	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
739	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
740	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
741	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
742	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
743	  function
744	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
745	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
746	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
747	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
748	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
749	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
750	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
751	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
752	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
753	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
754	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
755	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
756	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
757	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
758	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
759	\end{proof}

761	\subsection{The ambient subquotient induction for the Stacks~00K1 step}
762	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
763	
764	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
765	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
766	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction. Rather than build the
767	kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one element as graded modules
768	over a smaller quotient ring \(R/(x)\) -- which would force a graded structure on a
769	derived carrier -- we range the induction over \emph{pairs of homogeneous
770	submodules of a single fixed ambient graded module} \(M\). A pair \((N, N')\) with
771	\(N' \le N\) homogeneous submodules of \(M\) stands for the subquotient \(N/N'\); its
772	ambient Hilbert function is the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) -
773	\dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such pairs, equipped with finitely many
774	pairwise-commuting degree-one endomorphisms preserving \(N\) and \(N'\), is
775	\emph{closed} under the kernel and cokernel of one of those endomorphisms, both
776	again presented as ambient subquotient pairs. Consequently no graded quotient ring,
777	graded quotient module, or regrading of a subtype/quotient carrier is ever formed:
778	every object that appears is an ambient family \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\)
779	of submodules of the fixed \(M\).
780	
781	This subsection records that machinery as a thin layer over existing Mathlib
782	scaffold: Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
783	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of
784	homogeneous elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the degree-shift
785	of multiplication by a homogeneous element
786	(\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
787	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
788	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
789	What is supplied here is the internal direct-sum decomposition of a homogeneous
790	submodule as an ambient family (the split \(G_1\)), the subquotient Hilbert data,
791	the kernel/cokernel closure, the degreewise difference identity, the ambient
792	finiteness transfer, and the induction itself.
793	
794	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
795	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
796	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a graded
797	\(\kappa\)-vector space (the fixed ambient module) with finite-dimensional
798	components \(M_n\). All submodules \(N, N', \dots\) below are homogeneous submodules
799	of \(M\), and the relevant endomorphisms are \(\kappa\)-linear maps \(M \to M\)
800	raising degree by one -- typically multiplication by a homogeneous element
801	\(x \in R_1\).
802	
803	\subsubsection*{Mathlib dependency anchors}
804	
805	\begin{lemma}[Homogeneous submodule]
806	  \label{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
807	  \lean{Submodule.IsHomogeneous}
808	  \mathlibok
809	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
810	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Submodule}).}
811	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) an
812	  internally graded \(R\)-module. A submodule \(p \subseteq M\) is
813	  \emph{homogeneous} when it is closed under taking homogeneous components:
814	  \(m \in p\) implies each graded piece \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\). Equivalently
815	  \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) as a set. Mathlib bundles this into a homogeneous
816	  submodule type providing order and extensionality structure; the ambient internal
817	  direct-sum decomposition \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\) is supplied by the split
818	  \(G_1\) (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
819	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}).
820	\end{lemma}

826	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib

829	  whose elements is homogeneous. Then the ideal \(\mathrm{span}\,s\) is a
830	  homogeneous ideal. In particular, for \(x \in R_1\) the principal ideal \((x)\)
831	  is homogeneous, hence \(xM\) and the auxiliary submodule \(N' + x \cdot N\) are
832	  homogeneous; this homogeneity feeds the kernel/cokernel closure
833	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).

978	\subsubsection*{The internal grading of a homogeneous submodule (split $G_1$)}
979	
980	\begin{lemma}\leanok
981	[$G_1$ (independence) -- the family $n \mapsto M_n \cap p$ is independent]
982	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}
983	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_inf_iSupIndep}
984	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}

986	  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
987	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the family
988	  \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) of \(\kappa\)-subspaces of \(M\) is independent: for each
989	  \(n\),
990	  \[
991	    (M_n \cap p) \;\cap\; \Bigl(\bigsqcup_{m \ne n} (M_m \cap p)\Bigr) \;=\; 0 .
992	  \]
993	  The whole statement lives in the ambient module \(M\): the subspaces
994	  \(M_n \cap p\) are submodules of \(M\), never of a derived carrier \(\smash{\hat p}\).
995	\end{lemma}
996	
997	\begin{proof}
998	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
999	  \leanok
1000	  The components \(M_n\) of the internal decomposition of \(M\) are independent. The
1001	  subfamily \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) consists of submodules with \(M_n \cap p \le M_n\),
1002	  so independence is inherited: a relation among the \(M_n \cap p\) is in particular a
1003	  relation among the \(M_n\), forcing each term to vanish.
1004	\end{proof}

1006	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1007	[$G_1$ (supremum) -- $\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) = p$ for $p$ homogeneous]
1008	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}

1010	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}

1012	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then
1013	  \[
1014	    \bigsqcup_{n} (M_n \cap p) \;=\; p .
1015	  \]
1016	  Together with the independence of
1017	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}, this is the ambient internal
1018	  direct sum \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\), stated entirely inside \(M\) without
1019	  requiring a direct-sum decomposition of the subtype \(\smash{\hat p}\).

1022	\begin{proof}

1024	  \leanok
1025	  The inclusion \(\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) \le p\) is clear, since each
1026	  \(M_n \cap p \le p\). For the reverse inclusion, take \(m \in p\) and write
1027	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n\) using the
1028	  internal decomposition of \(M\). Homogeneity of \(p\) gives
1029	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\), hence \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n \cap p\); thus
1030	  \(m\) is a finite sum of elements of the \(M_n \cap p\), i.e.

1044	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e.\ \(M\) together with an
1045	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) whose components
1046	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
1047	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
1048	  \begin{itemize}
1049	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
1050	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
1051	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
1052	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
1053	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
1054	    \item a finiteness condition: the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a finite module over the
1055	      polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\) (via the
1056	      ambient \(t\)-stable carriers \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N\),
1057	      \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N'\) of \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}).
1058	  \end{itemize}
1059	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
1060	  Hilbert function} is
1061	  \[
1062	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
1063	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},
1064	  \]
1065	  the difference of the (finite) \(\kappa\)-dimensions of the ambient intersections,
1066	  computed in \(\mathbb{Z}\) and cast to \(\mathbb{Q}\). Since \(N' \le N\) one has
1067	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
1068	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
1069	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
1070	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
1071	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) guarantees that these ambient
1072	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
1073	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
1074	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).
1075	\end{definition}
1076	
1077	\subsubsection*{Ambient homogeneity calculus}
1078	
1079	The kernel/cokernel closure and the degreewise difference identity rest on a small
1080	calculus of how a degree-raising endomorphism interacts with the internal grading and
1081	with the lattice of homogeneous submodules. Throughout, \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
1082	is the fixed internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and \(x : M \to M\) is a
1083	\(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism raising degree by one. None of these statements forms a
1084	derived carrier: every object is a submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1085	
1086	\begin{definition}\leanok
1087	[Degree-raising endomorphism]
1088	  \label{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1089	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree}
1090	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module. A
1091	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism \(x : M \to M\) \emph{raises degree by one} when it
1092	  carries each graded piece into the next: \(x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1093	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) for every \(n\). This is the grading-ring-free form of
1094	  ``multiplication by a degree-one element'': it abstracts each of the \(r\)
1095	  commuting degree-one generators \(x \in R_1\) acting on \(M\) into a single
1096	  endomorphism, with no reference to the grading ring \(R\).
1097	\end{definition}
1098	
1099	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1100	[Membership form of degree-raising]
1101	  \label{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1102	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree.mem}
1103	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1104	  If \(x\) raises degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and \(m \in
1105	  \mathcal{M}_n\), then \(x m \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1106	\end{lemma}
1107	
1108	\begin{proof}
1109	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1110	  \leanok
1111	  Immediate from the definition: \(x m \in x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1112	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1113	\end{proof}
1114	
1115	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1116	[Degree-shift commutation]
1117	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1118	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree}
1119	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1120	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then for every \(m \in M\) and every \(i\) the
1121	  degree-\((i+1)\) homogeneous component of \(x m\) equals \(x\) applied to the
1122	  degree-\(i\) component of \(m\):
1123	  \[
1124	    \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) \;=\; x\bigl(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)\bigr) .
1125	  \]
1126	  This load-bearing commutation says a degree-raising endomorphism shifts the
1127	  internal decomposition by exactly one slot; it is what makes preimages and images
1128	  of homogeneous submodules under \(x\) homogeneous.
1129	\end{lemma}
1130	
1131	\begin{proof}
1132	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1133	  \leanok
1134	  Decompose \(m = \sum_j \mathrm{proj}_j(m)\) into its finitely many homogeneous
1135	  components and apply \(x\): \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with
1136	  \(x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m)) \in \mathcal{M}_{j+1}\) by
1137	  \cref{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}. Reading off the degree-\((i+1)\) component, all
1138	  terms with \(j+1 \ne i+1\) drop out, leaving \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\).
1139	\end{proof}
1140	
1141	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1142	[Degree-raising kills the bottom]
1143	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1144	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1145	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1146	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then the degree-\(0\) component of \(x m\) vanishes
1147	  for every \(m\): \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0\).
1148	\end{lemma}
1149	
1150	\begin{proof}
1151	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1152	  \leanok
1153	  Writing \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with each summand in
1154	  \(\mathcal{M}_{j+1}\), no summand has degree \(0\) (every \(j+1 \ge 1\)), so the
1155	  degree-\(0\) component is \(0\).
1156	\end{proof}
1157	
1158	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1159	[Preimage of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1160	  \label{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1161	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_isHomogeneous}
1162	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1163	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N' \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1164	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the preimage \(x^{-1}(N') =
1165	  \{m : x m \in N'\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of \(M\).
1166	\end{lemma}
1167	
1168	\begin{proof}
1169	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1170	  \leanok
1171	  Let \(m \in x^{-1}(N')\) and fix a degree \(i\). By the degree-shift commutation
1172	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}), \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)) =
1173	  \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m)\), which lies in \(N'\) because \(x m \in N'\) and \(N'\)
1174	  is homogeneous. Hence \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\), so \(x^{-1}(N')\) is
1175	  homogeneous.
1176	\end{proof}
1177	
1178	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1179	[Image of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1180	  \label{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1181	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_isHomogeneous}
1182	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1183	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1184	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule.
1185	  Then the image \(x \cdot N = \{x m : m \in N\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of
1186	  \(M\), with degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(x(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) and zero
1187	  degree-\(0\) piece.
1188	\end{lemma}
1189	
1190	\begin{proof}
1191	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1192	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1193	    \leanok
1194	  Take \(x m \in x \cdot N\) with \(m \in N\) and fix a degree. In degree \(0\) the
1195	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0 \in x \cdot N\)
1196	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}). In degree \(i+1\),
1197	  \(\mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\)
1198	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); since \(N\) is homogeneous,
1199	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in N\), so this component lies in \(x \cdot N\). Thus every
1200	  homogeneous component of \(x m\) lies in \(x \cdot N\), which is therefore
1201	  homogeneous.
1202	\end{proof}
1203	
1204	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1205	[Intersection of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1206	  \label{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}
1207	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.inf_isHomogeneous}
1208	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1209	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules
1210	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) then so is their intersection \(p
1211	  \cap q\). Mathlib provides no lattice-closure lemmas for homogeneous submodules, so
1212	  this is recorded here.
1213	\end{lemma}
1214	
1215	\begin{proof}
1216	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1217	  \leanok
1218	  For \(m \in p \cap q\) and any degree \(i\), homogeneity of \(p\) and of \(q\)
1219	  gives \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in q\), hence
1220	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p \cap q\).
1221	\end{proof}
1222	
1223	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1224	[Sum of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1225	  \label{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1226	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_isHomogeneous}
1227	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1228	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules then so is their sum \(p + q\).
1229	\end{lemma}
1230	
1231	\begin{proof}
1232	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1233	  \leanok
1234	  Write an element of \(p + q\) as \(a + b\) with \(a \in p\), \(b \in q\). For any
1235	  degree \(i\), \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) = \mathrm{proj}_i(a) + \mathrm{proj}_i(b)\)
1236	  with \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(b) \in q\) by homogeneity,
1237	  so \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) \in p + q\).
1238	\end{proof}
1239	
1240	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1241	[Ambient image identity]
1242	  \label{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}
1243	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_inf_degree_eq}
1244	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1245	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be homogeneous. Then the
1246	  degree-\((n+1)\) part of the image \(x \cdot N\) is exactly \(x\) of the
1247	  degree-\(n\) part of \(N\):
1248	  \[
1249	    (x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \;=\; x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) .
1250	  \]
1251	\end{lemma}
1252	
1253	\begin{proof}
1254	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1255	  \leanok
1256	  \(\supseteq\): for \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), \(x m \in x \cdot N\) and \(x m
1257	  \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) since \(x\) raises degree. \(\subseteq\): an element \(x m
1258	  \in x \cdot N\) lying in \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) equals its own degree-\((n+1)\)
1259	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_{n+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_n(m))\)
1260	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); homogeneity of \(N\) puts
1261	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so \(x m \in x \cdot (N \cap
1262	  \mathcal{M}_n)\). The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous by
1263	  \cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}.
1264	\end{proof}
1265	
1266	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1267	[Ambient distributive law]
1268	  \label{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1269	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_inf_degree_eq}
1270	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1271	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1272	  For homogeneous submodules \(P, Q \subseteq M\) and any degree \(k\), intersecting
1273	  the sum with a graded piece distributes:
1274	  \[
1275	    (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k \;=\; (P \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k) .
1276	  \]
1277	\end{lemma}
1278	
1279	\begin{proof}
1280	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1281	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1282	    \leanok
1283	  \(\supseteq\) is the general lattice inequality. For \(\subseteq\), take \(z = p +
1284	  q \in (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k\) with \(p \in P\), \(q \in Q\). Since \(z \in
1285	  \mathcal{M}_k\), \(z = \mathrm{proj}_k(z) = \mathrm{proj}_k(p) +
1286	  \mathrm{proj}_k(q)\); homogeneity of \(P\) and \(Q\)
1287	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) puts \(\mathrm{proj}_k(p) \in P \cap
1288	  \mathcal{M}_k\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_k(q) \in Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k\), so \(z \in (P
1289	  \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k)\). The intersection and sum stay in
1290	  the homogeneous lattice by \cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous} and
1291	  \cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}.
1292	\end{proof}
1293	
1294	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1295	
1296	The kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class is realised by eight ambient
1297	component facts: for a degree-raising endomorphism \(x\) and a homogeneous pair
1298	\(N' \le N\), the kernel pair is \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel pair is
1299	\((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\). The lemmas below record that both new pairs are homogeneous,
1300	nest correctly, are annihilated by \(x\), and are preserved by any endomorphism \(t\)
1301	commuting with \(x\) that preserves the original pair.
1302	
1303	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1304	[Homogeneity of the kernel pair's lower module]
1305	  \label{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}
1306	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_isHomogeneous}
1307	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1308	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and let \(N, N'\) be
1309	  homogeneous submodules of \(M\). Then the kernel pair's lower module
1310	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous. Indeed it is the intersection
1311	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous \(N\) with the homogeneous
1312	  preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}).
1313	\end{lemma}
1314	
1315	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1316	[Homogeneity of the cokernel pair's upper module]
1317	  \label{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}
1318	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_isHomogeneous}
1319	  \uses{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1320	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1321	  homogeneous: it is the sum (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous
1322	  \(N'\) with the homogeneous image \(x \cdot N\)
1323	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}).
1324	\end{lemma}
1325	
1326	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1327	[The kernel pair nests]
1328	  \label{lem:graded_ker_le}
1329	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_le}
1330	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N'\) (\(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)), then
1331	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed \(N' \le N\) by hypothesis, and \(x \cdot N'
1332	  \subseteq N'\) is exactly \(N' \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\); so \(N'\) is below the
1333	  intersection. Hence the kernel pair \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) is a valid
1334	  subquotient pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1335	\end{lemma}
1336	
1337	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1338	[The cokernel pair nests]
1339	  \label{lem:graded_coker_le}
1340	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_le}
1341	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N\) (\(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)), then
1342	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\): both summands lie in \(N\). Hence the cokernel pair
1343	  \((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) is a valid subquotient pair
1344	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1345	\end{lemma}
1346	
1347	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1348	[$x$ annihilates the kernel subquotient]
1349	  \label{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}
1350	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_annihilate}
1351	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries the kernel pair's lower module into \(N'\):
1352	  \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\). Indeed \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq
1353	  x^{-1}(N')\), and applying \(x\) to a preimage of \(N'\) lands in \(N'\). Thus \(x\)
1354	  acts as zero on the kernel subquotient.
1355	\end{lemma}
1356	
1357	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1358	[$x$ annihilates the cokernel subquotient]
1359	  \label{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}
1360	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_annihilate}
1361	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries \(N\) into the cokernel pair's upper module:
1362	  \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\), trivially (the right summand). Thus \(x\)
1363	  acts as zero on the cokernel subquotient \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\).
1364	\end{lemma}
1365	
1366	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1367	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the preimage]
1368	  \label{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_map_le_of_commute}
1370	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N'\) (\(t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)). Then
1371	  \(t\) preserves the preimage: \(t \cdot x^{-1}(N') \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed for
1372	  \(m\) with \(x m \in N'\) one has \(x(t m) = t(x m) \in t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\), so
1373	  \(t m \in x^{-1}(N')\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the kernel
1374	  pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\).
1375	\end{lemma}
1376	
1377	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1378	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the image]
1379	  \label{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1380	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_map_le_of_commute}
1381	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N\) (\(t \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then
1382	  \(t\) preserves the image: \(t \cdot (x \cdot N) \subseteq x \cdot N\). Indeed for
1383	  \(m \in N\) one has \(t(x m) = x(t m)\) with \(t m \in N\), so \(t(x m) \in x \cdot
1384	  N\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the cokernel pair's upper
1385	  module \(N' + x \cdot N\).
1386	\end{lemma}
1387	
1388	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1389	[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1390	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1391	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.ker}
1392	  % This is a narrative grouping lemma: its content is realised in Lean by the eight
1393	  % ambient component facts cited in \uses{} below (no single Lean declaration),
1394	  % together with the SubquotientDatum.ker/.coker constructors that bundle them into a
1395	  % length-(r-1) datum. The `\lean` pin names the `.ker` constructor — the single decl most
1396	  % representative of the bundled closure — so this dependency-bearing grouping node (which is
1397	  % `\uses`'d by the keystone rationality induction) stays wired into the graph rather than
1398	  % orphaning the component facts it `\uses`. This is a deliberate duplicate of the
1399	  % `def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker` pin.
1400	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1401	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1402	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1403	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1404	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1405	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1406	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1407	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1408	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1409	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1410	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1411	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1412	  \[
1413	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1414	    \qquad
1415	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1416	  \]
1417	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1418	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1419	  \begin{enumerate}
1420	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1421	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1422	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
