1424	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1425	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1426	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1427	  \end{enumerate}
1428	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1429	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1430	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1431	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1432	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1433	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1434	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1435	\end{lemma}

1437	\begin{proof}

1439	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,

1442	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1443	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1444	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1445	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1446	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1447	    \leanok
1448	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1449	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1450	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1451	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1452	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1453	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1454	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1455	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1456	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1457	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1458	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1459	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1460	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1461	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1462	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1463	  immediate. In the language of the ambient homogeneity calculus, this paragraph is
1464	  exactly: \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}),
1465	  \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}), and the
1466	  homogeneous lattice is closed under intersection
1467	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) and sum
1468	  (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}).
1469	
1470	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1471	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1472	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1473	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1474	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1475	
1476	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1477	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1478	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1479	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1480	
1481	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1482	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1483	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
1484	  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
1485	  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
1486	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
1487	\end{proof}

2417	  the \(\Gamma\)-pushforward dictionaries (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso},
2418	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}), the two pushforward-composition factors and the
2419	  congruence factor are transparent. The composition coherences collapse to the identity —
2420	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g',f})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) and
2421	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{f',g})^{\mathrm{inv}}\) evaluate, on the top open, to the identity
2422	  map on restriction of scalars (their section-level values are the identity) — and they
2423	  serve only to rebracket the iterated pushforward along the two factorizations
2424	  \((g' \circ f)_* = (f' \circ g)_*\) of the square's composite. (The
2425	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g',f})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) collapse is a transparent re-association
2426	  that is most naturally accounted for together with the leg-reindex of step~(iii) rather than at
2427	  this point; the \(\Gamma\)-collapse identity itself is the same.) The congruence factor collapses
2428	  to a canonical transport along the equality of opens: its section-level value
2429	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\,w)^{\mathrm{hom}}\) is the
2430	  presheaf-restriction along the canonical equality of opens, i.e.\ a repackaging along
2431	  that equality carrying no substantive content. Thus after the \(\Gamma\)-collapse the only
2432	  surviving non-trivial factor is \(f_{*}\bigl(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\bigr)\) read on global
2433	  sections.

2447	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}). Composing the surviving factor with the
2448	  codomain identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)

1602	\subsubsection*{The free polynomial-ring module structure}
1603	
1604	The finiteness condition of a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) and
1605	its transfer down one generator (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1606	phrased over the \emph{free} polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] =
1607	\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, \kappa)\). The following blocks build the
1608	action of that ring on the ambient module \(M\) from the \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1609	degree-raising endomorphisms \(t_i\), keeping every carrier an ambient submodule of
1610	\(M\). The free polynomial ring -- rather than the subalgebra
1611	\(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1612	-- is used precisely so the inductive transfer has the ring surjection
1613	\(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\) available
1614	(\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
1615	
1616	\begin{definition}\leanok
1617	[Polynomial evaluation ring homomorphism]
1618	  \label{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1619	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom}
1620	  \uses{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1621	  Let \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) be a family of
1622	  pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms. The \emph{polynomial evaluation
1623	  ring homomorphism}
1624	  \[
1625	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t) : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \longrightarrow
1626	      \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)
1627	  \]
1628	  sends each variable \(X_i\) to \(t_i\). It is constructed by evaluating into the
1629	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa(\{t_i\})
1630	  \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) -- commutative by
1631	  \cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib} because the generators commute -- and then
1632	  including \(A \hookrightarrow \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); evaluation into the
1633	  noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) directly is not an algebra
1634	  homomorphism, which is why the commutative \(A\) is interposed.
1635	\end{definition}
1636	
1637	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1638	[Evaluation sends $X_i$ to $t_i$]
1639	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1640	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_X}
1641	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1642	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) for each \(i\). Immediate from the construction
1643	  (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}), as evaluation sends \(X_i\) to the chosen image
1644	  \(t_i\).
1645	\end{lemma}
1646	
1647	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1648	[Evaluation sends a constant to a scalar]
1649	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1650	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_C}
1651	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1652	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\) for each \(c \in \kappa\):
1653	  the evaluation homomorphism (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}) is \(\kappa\)-linear and
1654	  carries the constant polynomial \(C\,c\) to the scalar endomorphism \(c \cdot 1\).
1655	\end{lemma}
1656	
1657	\begin{definition}\leanok
1658	[Polynomial-ring module structure on $M$]
1659	  \label{def:graded_polyModule}
1660	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule}
1661	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1662	  The \emph{polynomial-module structure} is the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module
1663	  structure on \(M\) obtained by restricting scalars along
1664	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)\) (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}): a polynomial \(p\) acts on
1665	  \(m \in M\) by \(p \cdot m = \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(p)(m)\). This is the module over the
1666	  free polynomial ring on which the ambient subquotient induction's finiteness condition
1667	  is measured.
1668	\end{definition}
1669	
1670	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1671	[$X_i$ acts as $t_i$]
1672	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}
1673	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_X_smul}
1674	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1675	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \(X_i \cdot m =
1676	  t_i(m)\) for all \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1677	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}).
1678	\end{lemma}
1679	
1680	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1681	[A constant acts as a scalar]
1682	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1683	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_C_smul}
1684	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1685	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \((C\,c) \cdot m =
1686	  c \cdot m\) for all \(c \in \kappa\), \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1687	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\)
1688	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1689	\end{lemma}
1690	
1691	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1692	[$\kappa$-scalar tower]
1693	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_isScalarTower}
1694	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_isScalarTower}
1695	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1696	  The polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}) is compatible with the
1697	  original \(\kappa\)-action: \(\mathrm{IsScalarTower}\,\kappa\,\kappa[t_0, \dots,
1698	  t_{r-1}]\,M\). Indeed the algebra map \(\kappa \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1699	  followed by the polynomial action recovers the \(\kappa\)-action, since a scalar
1700	  \(c \in \kappa\) maps to the constant \(C\,c\), which acts as \(c\)
1701	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1702	\end{lemma}
1703	
1704	\begin{definition}\leanok
1705	[Polynomial-ring submodule from a $t$-stable submodule]
1706	  \label{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1707	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule}
1708	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul,
1709	    lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1710	  Let \(N \subseteq M\) be a \(\kappa\)-submodule that is stable under each \(t_i\)
1711	  (\(t_i \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then \(N\), with the \emph{same} ambient carrier, is a
1712	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule of \(M\) for the polynomial-module structure
1713	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}). Stability under the action of an arbitrary polynomial
1714	  follows by induction on monomials: constants act as scalars
1715	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}) and multiplication by \(X_i\) acts as \(t_i\)
1716	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}), both of which preserve \(N\).
1717	\end{definition}
1718	
1719	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1720	[The polynomial submodule has the same carrier]
1721	  \label{lem:graded_polySubmodule_coe}
1722	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule_coe}
1723	  \uses{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1724	  The underlying set of \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}(t, N)\)
1725	  (\cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}) equals \(N\): the lift to a
1726	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule does not change the carrier, so every object
1727	  in the induction remains an ambient submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1728	\end{lemma}
1729	
1730	\subsubsection*{Finiteness-transfer infrastructure}
1731	
1732	The ambient finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer})
1733	factors the polynomial action of the larger ring through a surjection onto the
1734	smaller one. The following blocks build that surjection and the semilinearity
1735	identity it rests on, and record the two elementary finiteness manipulations
1736	(shrinking a numerator, enlarging a denominator) used to feed the kernel and
1737	cokernel constructors.
1738	
1739	\begin{definition}
1740	
1741	\leanok
1742	[The last-variable surjection of polynomial rings]
1743	  \label{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}
1744	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom}
1745	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_castSucc}
1746	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_last}
1747	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_C}
1748	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_rename_castSucc}
1749	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_surjective}
1750	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_ringHomSurjective}
1751	  \uses{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1752	  For a field \(\kappa\) and \(r \in \mathbb{N}\), the \emph{last-variable
1753	  homomorphism} is the \(\kappa\)-algebra map
1754	  \[
1755	    \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1756	      = \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r\}, \kappa)
1757	      \;\longrightarrow\;
1758	      \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r-1\}, \kappa) = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1759	  \]
1760	  obtained by evaluation: it sends the last variable \(X_r\) to \(0\) and each
1761	  earlier variable \(X_i\) (for \(0 \le i < r\)) to \(X_i\), and fixes constants.
1762	  It is a left inverse of the algebra map
1763	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1764	  induced by the canonical inclusion \(\{0, \dots, r-1\} \hookrightarrow \{0, \dots, r\}\)
1765	  on variable indices, and is therefore surjective. This is the concrete
1766	  ring surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1767	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) along which finiteness is transferred
1768	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
1769	\end{definition}
1770	
1771	\begin{lemma}
1772	
1773	\leanok
1774	[A $t$-stable submodule is closed under the polynomial action]
1775	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}
1776	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}
1777	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1778	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms
1779	  of \(M\) and let \(P' \subseteq M\) be a \(\kappa\)-submodule stable under each
1780	  \(t_i\) (\(t_i \cdot P' \subseteq P'\)). Then \(P'\) is closed under the action of
1781	  every polynomial: for all \(p \in \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) and \(m \in P'\),
1782	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(p)(m) \in P'\) (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}). The proof
1783	  is induction on \(p\): a constant acts as a scalar (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}),
1784	  multiplication by a variable acts as the stabilising \(t_i\)
1785	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}), and sums are handled additively.
1786	\end{lemma}
1787	
1788	\begin{lemma}
1789	
1790	\leanok
1791	[Mod-$P'$ semilinearity of the polynomial action]
1792	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}
1793	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}
1794	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C,
1795	    lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable, lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}
1796	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms of
1797	  \(M\), and let \(P' \le P\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules each stable under every
1798	  \(t_i\), with the last endomorphism \(x = t_r\) carrying \(P\) into \(P'\)
1799	  (\(x \cdot P \subseteq P'\)). Then for every polynomial
1800	  \(s \in \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) and every \(m \in P\),
1801	  \[
1802	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(s)(m)
1803	      \;-\; \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})
1804	        \bigl(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}(s)\bigr)(m)
1805	      \;\in\; P' .
1806	  \]
1807	  In words: acting by \(s\) through the full family \(t\) agrees, modulo \(P'\), with
1808	  first projecting away the last variable via \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}\)
1809	  (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}) and acting through the reduced family
1810	  \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\). This is the semilinearity identity that makes the
1811	  \(\sigma\)-semilinear quotient map of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1812	  well-defined. The proof is induction on \(s\): the case of an earlier variable
1813	  \(s = X_i\) (\(i < r\)) is the inductive hypothesis after \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}\)
1814	  fixes it; the case of the last variable \(s = X_r\) annihilates the reduced term,
1815	  and the full term lands in \(P'\) because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into the
1816	  \(t\)-stable \(P'\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_mem_of_stable}).
1817	\end{lemma}
1818	
1819	\begin{lemma}
1820	
1821	\leanok
1822	[Shrinking the numerator preserves finiteness]
1823	  \label{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1824	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1825	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1826	    lem:module_finite_of_injective_mathlib,
1827	    lem:mvPolynomial_isNoetherianRing_fin_mathlib,
1828	    lem:isNoetherian_of_isNoetherianRing_of_finite_mathlib}
1829	  Let \(N_1 \le N_2\) and \(P'\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1830	  the commuting family \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and suppose the subquotient
1831	  \(N_2/P'\) is finite over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1832	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}, \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(N_1/P'\) is
1833	  also finite over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\). Indeed the inclusion
1834	  \(N_1 \subseteq N_2\) induces an injective \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-linear
1835	  map \(N_1/P' \hookrightarrow N_2/P'\); since
1836	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) is Noetherian
1837	  (\cref{lem:mvPolynomial_isNoetherianRing_fin_mathlib}), the finite module
1838	  \(N_2/P'\) is Noetherian
1839	  (\cref{lem:isNoetherian_of_isNoetherianRing_of_finite_mathlib}), so its submodule

1843	\end{lemma}
1844	
1845	\begin{lemma}
1846	
1847	\leanok
1848	[Enlarging the denominator preserves finiteness]
1849	  \label{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
1850	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
1851	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1852	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}
1853	  Let \(N\) and \(P_1 \le P_2\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1854	  the commuting family \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and suppose the subquotient
1855	  \(N/P_1\) is finite over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1856	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}, \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(N/P_2\) is

1865	\subsubsection*{Ambient finiteness transfer}
1866	
1867	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1868	[Abstract single-pair finiteness transfer down one variable]
1869	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1870	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1871	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1872	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1873	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib,
1874	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1875	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms of
1876	  \(M\), and let \(P' \le P\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1877	  every \(t_i\). Suppose the last endomorphism \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\)
1878	  (\(x \cdot P \subseteq P'\)), and that the subquotient \(P/P'\) is finite over the
1879	  free polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) for the polynomial-module structure
1880	  of \cref{def:graded_polyModule} (with carriers the ambient \(t\)-stable submodules
1881	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\), \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\),
1882	  \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(P/P'\) is finite over
1883	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\).
1884	
1885	  This is the abstract single-pair transfer: it does not mention the kernel/cokernel
1886	  pairs, but is the engine applied to each of them by the kernel and cokernel
1887	  constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
1888	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}). Because the last variable
1889	  annihilates the subquotient, the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-action factors through
1890	  the last-variable surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1891	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), which is what makes the transfer drop one variable.
1892	\end{lemma}
1893	
1894	\begin{proof}
1895	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1896	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1897	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}
1898	    \leanok
1899	  Write \(\sigma = \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1900	  \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the surjective ring
1901	  homomorphism sending the last variable to \(0\) and each earlier variable \(X_i\)
1902	  (\(i < r\)) to \(X_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}). Consider the map on numerators
1903	  \[
1904	    g : \mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P \;\longrightarrow\;
1905	      \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/
1906	        \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr), \qquad y \longmapsto [\,y\,],
1907	  \]
1908	  where the source carries the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-module structure and the
1909	  target the reduced \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module structure (both with the
1910	  same ambient carrier \(P\), \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). The mod-\(P'\)
1911	  semilinearity identity (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}) -- acting by
1912	  \(s\) through the full family and acting by \(\sigma(s)\) through the reduced family
1913	  agree modulo \(P'\), precisely because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\) --
1914	  says exactly that \(g\) is \(\sigma\)-semilinear. The map \(g\) is surjective (it is
1915	  the quotient projection on the common carrier), and it kills the denominator
1916	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\), so it descends through that submodule
1917	  (\cref{lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}) to a surjective
1918	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-linear map

1921	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\;
1922	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1923	  \]
1924	  from the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finite subquotient onto the reduced one.
1925	  Finiteness transfers along this surjection
1926	  (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}), giving finiteness of \(P/P'\)
1927	  over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\).
1928	
1929	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1930	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), not the subalgebra
1931	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq
1932	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1933	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection
1934	  \(\sigma\) that the scalar transfer requires
1935	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}). Keeping free polynomial
1936	  rings at every step keeps \(\sigma\) -- and hence the transfer -- available
1937	  throughout. Everything is finite generation of ambient submodules of the fixed
1938	  \(M\) and their quotients.
1939	\end{proof}

2059	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the

1500	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1501	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1502	
1503	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1504	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1505	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1506	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1507	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1508	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1509	restricted sheaf over the base open. Neither is supplied directly by Mathlib: Mathlib
1510	does not provide the affine-local identification of \(\F|_W\) with the associated sheaf
1511	of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) together with the finiteness this transports.
1512	
1513	\begin{lemma}[G1 — finite-type quasi-coherent sheaf has finite affine sections]
1514	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1516	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1517	  % LEAN STATUS: G1 — project-built (mathlib-build target this iter); Mathlib has only
1518	  % the abstract SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType / IsQuasicoherent local-generator
1519	  % predicates, not this affine-local finiteness.
1520	  % NOTE: overlaps the QUOT keystone lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen — to be
1521	  % extracted to shared qcoh-affine-local infra (see STRATEGY Open questions).
1522	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB
1523	  % (Lemma "Characterizing modules of finite type", \S "Characterizing modules of
1524	  % finite type and finite presentation")
1525	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2092-L2098).
1526	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1527	  % "Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme.
1528	  %  The quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1529	  %  $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1530	  %  if and only if $M$ is a finite $R$-module."
1531	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1532	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of
1533	  modules with \(\F\) of finite type. Then for every affine open \(W \subseteq X\), the
1534	  section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1535	\end{lemma}
1536	
1537	\begin{proof}
1538	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1539	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (Tag 01PB, L2092-L2098;
1540	  % the Stacks proof reduces finite-type on a standard open cover to the algebra
1541	  % gluing lemma for finite generation of $M$ from finite generation of each $M_{f_i}$).
1542	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1543	  Fix an affine open \(W \subseteq X\) and write \(R = \Gamma(X, W)\) and
1544	  \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\). Identifying \(W\) with \(\Spec R\), quasi-coherence of \(\F\)
1545	  presents \(\F|_W\) as the associated sheaf \(\widetilde N\) of the \(R\)-module \(N\):
1546	  on a basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W\) the sections are the localized module
1547	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g)) = N_g\) over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = R_g\)
1548	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). The finite-type hypothesis on
1549	  \(\F\) says \(\F\) is locally generated by finitely many sections; restricted to the
1550	  quasi-compact affine \(W\), this yields a standard open cover
1551	  \(W = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} D(g_i)\) on each member of which \(\widetilde N|_{D(g_i)}\) is
1552	  generated by finitely many sections, i.e.\ each localization \(N_{g_i}\) is a finite
1553	  \(R_{g_i}\)-module. The elements \(g_1, \dots, g_n\) generate the unit ideal of \(R\)
1554	  (the \(D(g_i)\) cover \(\Spec R\)); by the standard finite-generation gluing
1555	  criterion — finite generation of \(N\) over \(R\) is local on such a cover — the
1556	  module \(N\) is finite over \(R\). Hence \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite
1557	  \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
1558	\end{proof}

1560	\begin{lemma}[G3 — flat-locality assembly from per-patch freeness]
1561	  \label{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1562	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1563	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1564	  % LEAN STATUS: G3 — project-built; the geometric glue identifying Γ(F, W∩W_j)
1565	  % localisations across the source cover and the base-restriction is the missing
1566	  % plumbing. Stub-level this iter; built after G1.
1567	  Let \(p : X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism with \(S\) integral and locally
1568	  noetherian, and \(\F\) a finite-type quasi-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Fix a non-empty
1569	  affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\) with \(A\) a noetherian domain, a finite
1570	  affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), and an element
1571	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that for every \(j\) the localized section module
1572	  \((M_j)_f\) (with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\)) is free over \(A_f\). Set
1573	  \(V = D(f) \subseteq U_0\). Then for every affine open \(U \le V\) of \(S\) and every
1574	  affine open \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), the section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat
1575	  over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).
1576	\end{lemma}
1577	
1578	\begin{proof}
1579	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1580	  Free modules are flat (\(\OO\)-flatness of a free module), so each \((M_j)_f\) is flat
1581	  over \(A_f\). Flatness of a module is local on the source: a module is flat over a
1582	  ring iff it is flat after localizing at every maximal ideal, and the family
1583	  \(\{W_j\}\) covers \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), so the per-patch flatness statements assemble to
1584	  flatness of the section module of \(\F\) over \(A_f = \Gamma(S, V)\) on the whole
1585	  preimage \(p^{-1}(V)\). The flat locus is moreover stable under base-restriction along
1586	  the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow V\): for an affine \(U \le V\) the ring
1587	  \(\Gamma(S, U)\) is a localization of \(\Gamma(S, V) = A_f\) (an open immersion of
1588	  affines into a basic open being a localization), and for an affine \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\)
1589	  the module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is the corresponding localization of the section module
1590	  over the relevant patch \(W_j\) (via \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1591	  identifying the finite section modules and their localizations across the cover).
1592	  Flatness is preserved under localization of both the ring and the module, so
1593	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over \(\Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
1594	\end{proof}

1601	\leanok
1602	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
1603	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
1604	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1605	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
1606	  % NOTE (re-signed): the Lean signature `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness`
1607	  % previously took `F : X.Modules` with NO coherence hypothesis, under which the
1608	  % statement is FALSE (a non-coherent module sheaf need not become flat on any
1609	  % non-empty open). The decl (not in archon-protected.yaml) was re-signed to carry
1610	  % the coherence encoding below; the Lean file is the source of truth for the exact
1611	  % binders and the full flatness conclusion.
1612	  %
1613	  % Mathlib has no single `IsCoherent` predicate at this pin; coherence over a
1614	  % locally noetherian base is encoded as the conjunction of two existing
1615	  % SheafOfModules predicates on `F`:
1616	  %     [F.IsQuasicoherent]   (SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent,
1617	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent)
1618	  %     [F.IsFiniteType]      (SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType,
1619	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Generators)
1620	  %
1621	  % LEAN SIGNATURE HEADER (matches the landed .lean file):
1622	  %   theorem genericFlatness {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral S] [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
1623	  %       (p : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType p] [QuasiCompact p] (F : X.Modules)
1624	  %       [F.IsQuasicoherent] [F.IsFiniteType] :
1625	  %       ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧ (the fibrewise flatness conclusion,
1626	  %       quantified over affine opens U ≤ V of S and affine opens W ≤ p⁻¹U of X, with
1627	  %       Γ(F,W) given its Γ(S,U)-module structure via p.appLE — verbatim form in the
1628	  %       .lean file, the source of truth).
1629	  %
1630	  % NOTE (iter-023): `[QuasiCompact p]` is essential — LocallyOfFiniteType does NOT
1631	  % entail QuasiCompact; without it the statement is false (counterexample:
1632	  % ⊔_i Spec ℤ → Spec ℤ with a finite-type torsion sheaf). It is the extra clause
1633	  % promoting `LocallyOfFiniteType` to `FiniteType`, and there is no instance
1634	  % `LocallyOfFiniteType → QuasiCompact`. The header above carries it, and the Step-1
1635	  % proof prose below invokes it directly (no false "finite type ⟹ quasi-compact" step).
1636	
1637	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1638	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1639	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1640	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1641	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1642	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1643	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1644	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1645	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1646	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1647	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1648	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1649	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1650	
1651	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1652	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1653	\end{theorem}
1654	
1655	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1656	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1657	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1658	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1659	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1660	% by restricting to a non-empty
1661	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1662	\begin{proof}
1663	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
1664	        lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1665	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1666	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1667	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1668	
1669	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact (by
1670	  the \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\,p]\) hypothesis) and \(U_0\) is affine hence
1671	  quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is quasi-compact; choose a
1672	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
1673	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
1674	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1675	
1676	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1677	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1678	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
1679	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
1680	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
1681	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
1682	
1683	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1684	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1685	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1686	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1687	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1688	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1689	
1690	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1691	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the flat-locality assembly
1692	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly} promotes these per-patch freeness statements to
1693	  the fibrewise flatness conclusion, via the criterion that a module flat at every
1694	  maximal ideal is flat together with the locality of flatness on the source cover. This
1695	  produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1696	\end{proof}

1698	\section{Mathlib status}
1699	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
1700	
1701	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
1702	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
1703	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
1704	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
1705	generic-flatness argument consumes
1706	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
1707	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
1708	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
1709	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
1710	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
1711	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1712	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
1713	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
1714	are introduced in
1715	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.

2461	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
2462	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2463	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
2464	  \mathlibok
2465	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2466	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
2467	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
2468	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
2469	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
2470	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
2471	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
2472	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
2473	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
2474	\end{lemma}

2476	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
2477	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2478	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
2479	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib, lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization,
2480	    lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
2481	    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
2482	    lem:qcoh_pullback_fromSpec, lem:modules_restrict_linear,
2483	    lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen_linear, lem:fromSpec_image_top_section_coherence,
2484	    lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general, lem:isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_hP1}
2485	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
2486	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
2487	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
2488	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2489	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
2490	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
2491	  %  associated to $M$.
2492	  %  [...]
2493	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
2494	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
2495	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
2496	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
2497	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
2498	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
2499	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
2500	  \begin{enumerate}
2501	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
2502	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
2503	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
2504	    \item the section-restriction map
2505	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
2506	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
2507	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
2508	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
2509	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
2510	  \end{enumerate}
2511	\end{lemma}
2512	
2513	\begin{proof}
2514	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib, lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization,
2515	    lem:qcoh_pullback_fromSpec, lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
2516	    lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general, lem:fromSpec_image_top_section_coherence,
2517	    lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen_linear, def:gamma_image_ring_equiv,
2518	    lem:isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_hP1}
2519	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2520	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2521	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
2522	
2523	  Part (2) is the \emph{gap-2 transport}. Because \(U\) is affine, Mathlib's
2524	  canonical affine immersion \(\mathrm{fromSpec} : \Spec \Gamma(X, U) \to X\) has
2525	  image \(U\) and identifies \(U\) with the spectrum of its ring of sections. We
2526	  obtain (2) by instantiating the general-ambient-scheme transport core
2527	  \cref{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general} at the affine open immersion
2528	  \(j := \mathrm{fromSpec}\), then bridging its conclusion to the basic-open
2529	  restriction.
2530	
2531	  \emph{Supplying the P1 datum.} The core requires
2532	  \(\mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(((\mathrm{pullback}\,\mathrm{fromSpec}).\mathrm{obj}\,
2533	  \mathcal{M}).\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\bigr)\). This is produced in two steps: first the
2534	  pullback \((\mathrm{pullback}\,\mathrm{fromSpec}).\mathrm{obj}\,\mathcal{M}\) is
2535	  again quasi-coherent by \cref{lem:qcoh_pullback_fromSpec} (quasi-coherence is
2536	  preserved under pullback along \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\)); then, since this pullback
2537	  lives on the affine scheme \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\),
2538	  \cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ} turns that quasi-coherence into the
2539	  required isomorphism of the \(\widetilde{(-)}\)-counit.
2540	
2541	  \emph{Applying the core.} Feeding this P1 datum, the element \(f\), and the slice
2542	  element \(f' := \sigma^{-1}(f)\) --- where
2543	  \(\sigma = (\Gamma\Spec\text{-iso})^{-1} \circ
2544	  \mathrm{gammaImageRingEquiv}\,\mathrm{fromSpec}\,\top\)
2545	  (\cref{def:gamma_image_ring_equiv}) is the \(\top\)-level section ring iso, so the
2546	  transport hypothesis \(\sigma(f') = f\) holds by construction --- to
2547	  \cref{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general} yields that the section
2548	  restriction
2549	  \(\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, \mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,\top) \to
2550	  \Gamma(\mathcal{M}, \mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,D(f'))\) is
2551	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(\Gamma(X, U)\).
2552	
2553	  \emph{Bridging to the basic-open restriction.} It remains to identify this
2554	  statement, posed on the image opens \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,\top\) and
2555	  \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,D(f')\), with
2556	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) for the consumer-facing
2557	  basic-open restriction \(\mathrm{restrictBasicOpenₗ}\)
2558	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen_linear}). The image opens are
2559	  \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,\top = U\) and
2560	  \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,D(f') = D(f)\). The one nontrivial coherence is that,
2561	  under the identification \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,\top = U\), the section ring
2562	  iso \(\sigma\) restricts to the identity --- precisely \(\rho(\sigma\,f') = f\),
2563	  where \(\rho\) is the presheaf transport along that open identification --- which
2564	  is exactly the section-coherence crux
2565	  \cref{lem:fromSpec_image_top_section_coherence}. With it the localized-module
2566	  property transports across the open identification isomorphisms to the
2567	  basic-open restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\), establishing (2).
2568	\end{proof}

2570	\begin{lemma}
2571	[Basic-open localization given the pullback-$\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}$ isomorphism datum]
2572	  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_hP1}
2573	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_hP1}
2574	  \uses{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general, lem:fromSpec_image_top_section_coherence,
2575	    lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen_linear, lem:isLocalizedModule_ringEquiv_semilinear,
2576	    def:gamma_image_ring_equiv}
2577	  \textit{The $\mathrm{hP1}$-explicit form of \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} part (2):
2578	  the basic-open restriction localizes when the quasi-coherence of the pullback is given as a hypothesis,
2579	  and the proof reduces to a purely mechanical open/ring-iso bridge.}
2580	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\), \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open with
2581	  canonical affine immersion \(\mathrm{fromSpec} : \Spec \Gamma(X,U) \to X\), and \(f \in \Gamma(X,U)\).
2582	  Suppose
2583	  \(\mathrm{hP1} : \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(((\mathrm{pullback}\,\mathrm{fromSpec}).\mathrm{obj}\,
2584	  \mathcal{M}).\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\bigr)\). Then the basic-open restriction
2585	  \(\mathrm{restrictBasicOpenₗ}\,\mathcal{M}\,f : \Gamma(\mathcal{M}, U) \to
2586	  \Gamma(\mathcal{M}, X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f)\) is \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over
2587	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\).
2588	\end{lemma}
2589	\begin{proof}
2590	  \uses{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general, lem:fromSpec_image_top_section_coherence,
2591	    lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen_linear, lem:isLocalizedModule_ringEquiv_semilinear,
2592	    def:gamma_image_ring_equiv}
2593	  Instantiate the general-ambient transport core \cref{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general} at the
2594	  affine open immersion \(j := \mathrm{fromSpec}\) (its base ring is then \(\Gamma(X,U)\)), with slice
2595	  element \(f' := \sigma^{-1}(f)\) where \(\sigma = \mathrm{gammaImageRingEquiv}\,j\,\top\)
2596	  (\cref{def:gamma_image_ring_equiv}); the transport hypothesis \(\sigma(f') = f\) holds by construction.
2597	  Fed the datum \(\mathrm{hP1}\), the core yields \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f')\) for
2598	  the restriction \(\mathrm{restrictₗ}\,\mathcal{M} : \Gamma(\mathcal{M}, j\,''^{U}\top) \to
2599	  \Gamma(\mathcal{M}, j\,''^{U}D(f'))\) over \(A := \Gamma(X, j\,''^{U}\top)\). Transport this to the
2600	  consumer-facing basic-open restriction across: (i) the open identifications \(j\,''^{U}\top = U\) and
2601	  \(j\,''^{U}D(f') = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) (the latter Mathlib's
2602	  \(\mathrm{IsAffineOpen.fromSpec\_image\_basicOpen}\)); (ii) the section ring iso \(\rho : A \cong
2603	  \Gamma(X,U)\) induced by \(j\,''^{U}\top = U\), for which \(\rho(\sigma f') = f\) is the section-coherence
2604	  crux \cref{lem:fromSpec_image_top_section_coherence}; and (iii) the semilinear localization-transfer
2605	  lemma \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_ringEquiv_semilinear} along \(\rho\), with
2606	  \((\mathrm{powers}\,f').\mathrm{map}\,\rho = \mathrm{powers}\,f\) by \(\mathrm{Submonoid.map\_powers}\).
2607	  This yields \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) for
2608	  \(\mathrm{restrictBasicOpenₗ}\,\mathcal{M}\,f\) (\cref{lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen_linear}).
2609	\end{proof}

2613	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2614	[$\Gamma(X,U)$-linear section restriction]
2615	  \label{lem:modules_restrict_linear}
2616	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.restrictₗ}
2617	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let \(V \subseteq U\) be an inclusion
2618	  of opens. The presheaf restriction \(\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, U) \to \Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)\) is
2619	  \(\Gamma(X, U)\)-linear, where the codomain \(\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)\) carries the
2620	  \(\Gamma(X, U)\)-module structure obtained by restricting scalars along the structure-sheaf
2621	  restriction \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma(X, V)\).
2622	\end{lemma}
2623	\begin{proof}
2624	  Additivity is automatic. Compatibility with scalar multiplication is the semilinearity of presheaf
2625	  restriction for a sheaf of modules: restriction commutes with the \(\Gamma(X, U)\)-action, which over
2626	  the restrict-scalars codomain is exactly \(\Gamma(X, U)\)-linearity.
2627	\end{proof}

2644	  semilinearity of presheaf restriction together with the scalar-tower compatibility relating the
2645	  \(\Gamma(X, U)\)- and \(\Gamma(X, X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f)\)-actions, using that the algebra map

2650	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2651	[Section coherence of the affine immersion $\mathrm{fromSpec}$]
2652	  \label{lem:fromSpec_image_top_section_coherence}
2653	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.fromSpec_image_top_section_coherence}
2654	  \uses{def:gamma_image_ring_equiv}
2655	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, with canonical affine immersion
2656	  \(\mathrm{fromSpec} : \Spec \Gamma(X, U) \to X\) of image \(U\), and write
2657	  \(e_{\top} : \mathrm{fromSpec}\,''^{U}\,\top = U\) for the canonical identification of its image
2658	  with \(U\). Let
2659	  \(\sigma = (\Gamma\Spec\text{-iso})^{-1} \circ \mathrm{gammaImageRingEquiv}\,\mathrm{fromSpec}\,\top\)
2660	  (\cref{def:gamma_image_ring_equiv}) be the \(\top\)-level section ring isomorphism, and let
2661	  \(\rho : \mathcal{O}_X(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X(\mathrm{fromSpec}''^{U}\,\top)\) be the
2662	  structure-sheaf isomorphism induced by \(e_{\top}\). Then \(\rho \circ \sigma = \mathrm{id}\), i.e.,
2663	  \(\rho(\sigma\,f) = f\) for every \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\).
2664	\end{lemma}
2665	\begin{proof}
2666	  The composite \(\rho \circ \sigma\) is a ring endomorphism of \(\Gamma(X, U)\). Expanding the
2667	  definition of \(\sigma\) via \cref{def:gamma_image_ring_equiv} and applying the self-application
2668	  identity of \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\) over \(U\)---which identifies the image of the total open of
2669	  \(\Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) under \(\mathrm{fromSpec}\) with \(U\)---together with presheaf naturality,
2670	  the composite \(\rho \circ \sigma\) reduces to a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra endomorphism of
2671	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\). Since the only such endomorphism is the identity, \(\rho(\sigma\,f) = f\).
2672	\end{proof}

2674	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2675	[Basic-open Hfr along an abstract affine open immersion, general ambient scheme]
2676	  \label{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux_general}
2677	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.section_localization_hfr_aux_general}
2678	  \uses{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ, lem:gamma_pullback_image_iso,
2679	    lem:gamma_pullback_image_iso_hom_semilinear, lem:gamma_pullback_image_iso_hom_naturality,
2680	    def:gamma_image_ring_equiv, lem:isLocalizedModule_ringEquiv_semilinear,
2681	    lem:modules_restrict_linear}
2682	  This is the general-ambient-scheme analogue of \cref{lem:section_localization_hfr_aux}: the ambient
2683	  scheme \(X\) need not be affine, and the localization ring is the section ring
2684	  \(\Gamma(X, j\,''^{U}\,\top)\) of the image open.
2685	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and \(j : \Spec S \to X\) an affine open
2686	  immersion with
2687	  \(\mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(((\mathrm{pullback}\,j).\mathrm{obj}\,\mathcal{M}).\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\bigr)\)
2688	  (the P1 datum). Fix a slice element \(f' \in S\) and a section \(f \in \Gamma(X, j\,''^{U}\,\top)\) with
2689	  \(\mathrm{gammaImageRingEquiv}\,j\,\top\,((\Gamma\Spec\text{-iso}_S)^{-1} f') = f\). Then the section
2690	  restriction \(\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, j\,''^{U}\,\top) \to \Gamma(\mathcal{M}, j\,''^{U}\,D(f'))\) is
2691	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(A := \Gamma(X, j\,''^{U}\,\top)\), the
2692	  conclusion map being \(\mathrm{restrictₗ}\) of \cref{lem:modules_restrict_linear}.
2693	\end{lemma}
2694	\begin{proof}
2695	  \uses{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ, lem:gamma_pullback_image_iso,
2696	    lem:gamma_pullback_image_iso_hom_semilinear, lem:gamma_pullback_image_iso_hom_naturality,
2697	    def:gamma_image_ring_equiv, lem:isLocalizedModule_ringEquiv_semilinear}
2698	  Set \(M' = (\mathrm{pullback}\,j).\mathrm{obj}\,\mathcal{M}\); the P1 hypothesis gives
2699	  \(\mathrm{IsIso}\,M'.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\), so \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
2700	  localizes the slice restriction \(\Gamma(M', \top) \to \Gamma(M', D(f'))\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f'\)
2701	  over the slice ring \(S\). The \(\sigma\)-semilinear additive isomorphisms
2702	  \(e_1 = \mathrm{gammaPullbackImageIso}\,j\,\mathcal{M}\,\top\) and
2703	  \(e_2 = \mathrm{gammaPullbackImageIso}\,j\,\mathcal{M}\,D(f')\) (\cref{lem:gamma_pullback_image_iso}),
2704	  over the section ring iso
2705	  \(\sigma = (\Gamma\Spec\text{-iso}_S)^{-1} \circ \mathrm{gammaImageRingEquiv}\,j\,\top\)
2706	  (\cref{def:gamma_image_ring_equiv}), are semilinear by
2707	  \cref{lem:gamma_pullback_image_iso_hom_semilinear} and intertwine the slice localization map with the
2708	  section restriction by \cref{lem:gamma_pullback_image_iso_hom_naturality}. Bridge (I)
2709	  \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_ringEquiv_semilinear} transports the localization across \(\sigma\),
2710	  landing \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\) at \((\mathrm{powers}\,f').\mathrm{map}\,\sigma\); since
2711	  \(\sigma(f') = f\) and \(\sigma\) carries powers to powers (ring maps preserve the submonoid of powers),
2712	  this submonoid is \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). Because here the base and
2713	  target rings coincide (\(A = \Gamma(X, j\,''^{U}\,\top)\) is itself the localization ring), no further
2714	  restriction-of-scalars bridge is needed, and the conclusion is
2715	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(A\) for the restriction \(\mathrm{restrictₗ}\).
2716	\end{proof}

2720	\begin{definition}[Inverse of the slice equivalence sends the unit module to the unit module]
2721	  \label{def:over_restrict_unit_iso_inv}
2722	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictUnitIsoInv}
2723	  \uses{def:over_restrict_equiv, lem:isIso_unitToPushforwardObjUnit_of_isIso}
2724	  \textit{The inverse of the slice equivalence functor preserves the structure-sheaf (unit)
2725	  module; dual to \cref{def:over_restrict_unit_iso}.}
2726	  Let \(V \subseteq X\) be an open subscheme. The \emph{inverse} functor of the slice
2727	  equivalence (\cref{def:over_restrict_equiv}) carries the unit (structure-sheaf) module \(\mathbf{1}\)
2728	  of \(V.\mathrm{toScheme}.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf}\) to the unit module of the over-site
2729	  \(X.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf}.\mathrm{over}\,V\):
2730	  \[
2731	    (\mathrm{overRestrictEquiv}\,V).\mathrm{inverse}.\mathrm{obj}\,
2732	      \bigl(\mathbf{1}\,(V.\mathrm{toScheme}.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf})\bigr)
2733	      \;\cong\; \mathbf{1}\,(X.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf}.\mathrm{over}\,V) .
2734	  \]
2735	\end{definition}
2736	\begin{proof}
2737	  \uses{def:over_restrict_equiv, lem:isIso_unitToPushforwardObjUnit_of_isIso}
2738	  As in the forward case (\cref{def:over_restrict_unit_iso}), the inverse functor of the equivalence is
2739	  the pushforward \(\mathrm{pushforward}\,\varphi\) of sheaves of modules along an over-site/open-site
2740	  ring comparison \(\varphi\) built from the (op of the) unit isomorphism of
2741	  \((\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,V)\); since that unit is an isomorphism, \(\varphi\) is an
2742	  isomorphism. The canonical unit comparison \(\mathrm{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\varphi\) is then an
2743	  isomorphism by the \(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi\)-driven criterion
2744	  (\cref{lem:isIso_unitToPushforwardObjUnit_of_isIso}), and its inverse is the asserted isomorphism.
2745	\end{proof}

2747	\begin{definition}[Geometric presentation back-transported to a slice presentation]
2748	  \label{def:over_restrict_presentation_inv}
2749	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictPresentationInv}
2750	  \uses{def:over_restrict_unit_iso_inv, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso, lem:presentation_map_mathlib}
2751	  \textit{Dual to \cref{def:over_restrict_presentation}: a presentation of the geometric pullback
2752	  yields a presentation of the abstract Grothendieck slice.}
2753	  Let \(V \subseteq X\) be open and \(M\) a sheaf of modules on \(X\). A presentation of the geometric
2754	  pullback \((V.\iota^{*})\,M\) yields a presentation of the abstract Grothendieck-slice restriction
2755	  \(M.\mathrm{over}\,V\):
2756	  \[
2757	    \bigl((V.\iota^{*})\,M\bigr).\mathrm{Presentation}
2758	      \;\longrightarrow\;
2759	    (M.\mathrm{over}\,V).\mathrm{Presentation} .
2760	  \]
2761	\end{definition}
2762	\begin{proof}
2763	  \uses{def:over_restrict_unit_iso_inv, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso, lem:presentation_map_mathlib}
2764	  First transport the given presentation of \((V.\iota^{*})\,M\) across the inverse of the pullback
2765	  packaging isomorphism \((\mathrm{overRestrictEquiv}\,V).\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,
2766	  (M.\mathrm{over}\,V) \cong (V.\iota^{*})\,M\) (\cref{lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso}) via
2767	  \(\mathrm{Presentation.ofIsIso}\), obtaining a presentation of the transported slice module
2768	  \((\mathrm{overRestrictEquiv}\,V).\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,(M.\mathrm{over}\,V)\). Then apply
2769	  \(\mathrm{Presentation.map}\) (\cref{lem:presentation_map_mathlib}) along the inverse functor
2770	  \((\mathrm{overRestrictEquiv}\,V).\mathrm{inverse}\), using \cref{def:over_restrict_unit_iso_inv} to
2771	  match the image of the structure-sheaf (unit) module with the target unit module; this lands a
2772	  presentation of \((\mathrm{overRestrictEquiv}\,V).\mathrm{inverse}.\mathrm{obj}\,
2773	  \bigl((\mathrm{overRestrictEquiv}\,V).\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,(M.\mathrm{over}\,V)\bigr)\).
2774	  Finally collapse the round trip across the counit isomorphism of the equivalence
2775	  (\(\mathrm{inverse} \circ \mathrm{functor} \cong \mathrm{id}\)) via \(\mathrm{Presentation.ofIsIso}\),
2776	  giving a presentation of \(M.\mathrm{over}\,V\).
2777	\end{proof}

2779	\begin{definition}[Presentation of the pullback's geometric restriction to a preimage cover member]
2780	  \label{def:presentation_pullback_iota_preimage}
2781	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.presentationPullbackιPreimage}
2782	  \uses{def:presentation_pullback_iota_of_quasicoherentData, lem:presentation_map_mathlib,
2783	    lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}
2784	  \textit{For an open immersion $g$, the pulled-back sheaf has a presentation on the geometric
2785	  restriction to each preimage cover member.}
2786	  Let \(g : Y \to X\) be an open immersion, \(M\) a sheaf of modules on \(X\) with quasi-coherence data
2787	  \(q\), and \(i\) a cover index. Write \(N := (\mathrm{pullback}\,g).\mathrm{obj}\,M\) and
2788	  \(W_i := g^{-1}(q.X_i)\). Then the geometric restriction
2789	  \((W_i.\iota^{*})\,N\) admits a \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.Presentation}\) on \(W_i.\mathrm{toScheme}\).
2790	\end{definition}
2791	\begin{proof}
2792	  \uses{def:presentation_pullback_iota_of_quasicoherentData, lem:presentation_map_mathlib,
2793	    lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}
2794	  The open immersion \(g\) restricts to an open immersion
2795	  \(k : W_i.\mathrm{toScheme} \to (q.X_i).\mathrm{toScheme}\) on preimages, characterized by the
2796	  commuting square \(k \circ (q.X_i).\iota = W_i.\iota \circ g\) (the morphism induced by \(g\) between
2797	  the inclusion of \(W_i = g^{-1}(q.X_i)\) and that of \(q.X_i\)). By pseudofunctoriality of pullback
2798	  (\(\mathrm{pullbackComp}\), named in prose) this square gives a natural isomorphism
2799	  \[
2800	    (W_i.\iota^{*})\,N \;=\; (W_i.\iota^{*})\,(g^{*}M)
2801	      \;\cong\; (W_i.\iota \circ g)^{*}M
2802	      \;=\; (k \circ (q.X_i).\iota)^{*}M
2803	      \;\cong\; k^{*}\bigl((q.X_i).\iota^{*}\,M\bigr) .
2804	  \]
2805	  Now \((q.X_i).\iota^{*}\,M\) carries the geometric presentation
2806	  \cref{def:presentation_pullback_iota_of_quasicoherentData}. Apply \(\mathrm{Presentation.map}\)
2807	  (\cref{lem:presentation_map_mathlib}) along the pullback functor \(k^{*}\)
2808	  (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}), which preserves colimits and carries the unit sheaf to the
2809	  unit sheaf, to obtain a presentation of \(k^{*}\bigl((q.X_i).\iota^{*}\,M\bigr)\); transport it across
2810	  the displayed isomorphism via \(\mathrm{Presentation.ofIsIso}\) to land a presentation of
2811	  \((W_i.\iota^{*})\,N\).
2812	\end{proof}

2818	    lem:presentation_isQuasicoherent_mathlib}
2819	  \textit{The abstract Grothendieck slice of the pullback over a preimage cover member is
2820	  quasi-coherent.}
2821	  With \(g : Y \to X\) an open immersion, \(M\) quasi-coherent with datum \(q\),

2823	  restriction \(N.\mathrm{over}\,W_i\) is quasi-coherent.

2835	\begin{lemma}[The preimage family of a quasi-coherence cover covers the source]
2836	  \label{lem:coversTop_preimage}
2837	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.coversTop_preimage}
2838	  \uses{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib}
2839	  \textit{Preimages of a top cover under a morphism again cover the top.}
2840	  Let \(g : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and \(q\) quasi-coherence data for a sheaf \(M\) on
2841	  \(X\), with cover \(\{q.X_i\}\) of \(X\) (\cref{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib}). Then
2842	  the preimage family \(\{\,W_i := g^{-1}(q.X_i)\,\}\) is a \(\mathrm{CoversTop}\) family on \(Y\).
2843	\end{lemma}
2844	\begin{proof}
2845	  \uses{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib}
2846	  The covering property \(q.\mathrm{coversTop}\) says \(\{q.X_i\}\) generates the maximal sieve on
2847	  \(\top_X\); pulling back along the continuous map underlying \(g\) commutes with the sieve operations
2848	  and sends \(\top_X\) to \(\top_Y\) (\(g^{-1}\top = \top\) and \(g^{-1}\) preserves suprema), so the
2849	  preimage family \(\{g^{-1}(q.X_i)\}\) generates the maximal sieve on \(\top_Y\), i.e.\ it covers the
2850	  top.
2851	\end{proof}

2897	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

2899	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, item (4); Spec-local core of

2904	  exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, D(f))\) as the localized module

2912	  \(N \to \Gamma(\widetilde N, \top)\) is an isomorphism and the canonical map to \(D(f)\) factors
2913	  through it followed by the restriction, precomposing the localization map with the inverse
2914	  isomorphism transfers the \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\) property to the restriction map
2915	  (Mathlib's transfer lemma for localization along a linear equivalence).

1311	its compatibility \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) with the chart projections. We record both
1312	as the next layer of bridge results; their cocycle coherence is the residual
1313	obligation discharged in \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}.
1314	
1315	\begin{definition}
1316	
1317	\leanok
1318	[The triple-overlap transition \(t'\)]
1319	  \label{def:gr_chart_transition'}
1320	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition'}
1321	  \uses{def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}
1322	  The \(t'\)-field of the Grassmannian glue datum is the morphism of schemes
1323	  \[
1324	    t'_{I,J,K} : U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K \longrightarrow U^J_K \times_{U^J} U^J_I ,
1325	  \]
1326	  defined as the composite
1327	  \[
1328	    U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K
1329	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \Spec S_I
1330	      \xrightarrow{\ \Spec \Theta_{I,J}\ } \Spec S_J
1331	      \xrightarrow{\ \Spec \mathrm{swap}\ } \Spec R^J[1/(P^J_K P^J_I)]
1332	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } U^J_K \times_{U^J} U^J_I ,
1333	  \]
1334	  where the first and last arrows are the pullback identifications of
1335	  \cref{def:gr_away_pullback_iso} -- the source one for the pair \((P^I_J, P^I_K)\)
1336	  over \(R^I\), and the target one (taken inverse) for the pair \((P^J_K, P^J_I)\)
1337	  over \(R^J\) -- the middle arrow is the \(\Spec\) of the localised
1338	  triple-overlap transition \(\Theta_{I,J} : S_J \to S_I\)
1339	  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}), and \(\mathrm{swap}\) is the \(\Spec\) of the
1340	  order-swap isomorphism \(R^J[1/(P^J_K P^J_I)] \cong S_J = R^J[1/(P^J_I P^J_K)]\)
1341	  (\cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}). The order-swap is inserted precisely to
1342	  reconcile the product order \(P^J_K P^J_I\) carried by the target pullback
1343	  identification with the order \(P^J_I P^J_K\) of the codomain \(S_J\) of
1344	  \(\Theta_{I,J}\).
1345	\end{definition}
1346	
1347	\begin{lemma}
1348	
1349	\leanok
1350	[The \(t'\) ring identity]
1351	  \label{lem:gr_chartTransition'_ringIdentity}
1352	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition'_ringIdentity}
1353	  \uses{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_away_incl_left, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap, lem:mathlib_away_lift}
1354	  Over the triple-overlap rings, the localised transition \(\Theta_{I,J}\)
1355	  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}) pre-composed with the order-swap and the right
1356	  inclusion equals the left inclusion post-composed with the plain transition
1357	  \(\theta_{I,J}\), as ring homomorphisms \(R^J[1/P^J_I] \to S_I\):
1358	  \[
1359	    \Theta_{I,J} \circ \mathrm{swap} \circ \iota^{\mathrm{R}}_{P^J_K, P^J_I}
1360	      \;=\; \iota^{\mathrm{L}}_{P^I_J, P^I_K} \circ \theta_{I,J} .
1361	  \]
1362	\end{lemma}
1363	\begin{proof}
1364	  \uses{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, def:gr_away_incl_right, def:gr_away_incl_left, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap, lem:mathlib_away_lift}
1365	  \leanok
1366	  Both sides are ring maps out of the away-localisation \(R^J[1/P^J_I]\), so by the
1367	  universal property of the localisation (\cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}) it suffices
1368	  to check equality after pre-composing with the structure map
1369	  \(R^J \to R^J[1/P^J_I]\), i.e.\ on the chart ring \(R^J\). There the right
1370	  inclusion and the order-swap collapse to structure maps
1371	  (\cref{lem:gr_awayInclRight_comp_algebraMap},
1372	  \cref{lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap}), and the lift defining
1373	  \(\Theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}) unwinds to
1374	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}} \circ \tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
1375	  (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}); the right side likewise unwinds through the lift
1376	  defining \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) to the same
1377	  \(\iota^{\mathrm{L}} \circ \tilde\theta_{I,J}\). Proved directly in Lean.
1378	\end{proof}
1379	
1380	\begin{lemma}
1381	
1382	\leanok
1383	[The \(t'\) compatibility field \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\)]
1384	  \label{lem:gr_chartTransition'_fac}
1385	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition'_fac}
1386	  \uses{def:gr_chart_transition', lem:gr_chartTransition'_ringIdentity, def:gr_away_pullback_iso, lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst, lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd, def:gr_chart_transition}
1387	  The triple-overlap transition \(t'\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}) is
1388	  compatible with the projections to the charts:
1389	  \[
1390	    \mathrm{pr}_2 \circ t'_{I,J,K} \;=\; t_{I,J} \circ \mathrm{pr}_1 ,
1391	  \]
1392	  where \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) is the first projection of the source pullback
1393	  \(U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K\), \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) the second projection of the
1394	  target pullback \(U^J_K \times_{U^J} U^J_I\), and \(t_{I,J}\) the scheme-level
1395	  transition (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}).
1396	\end{lemma}
1397	\begin{proof}
1398	  \uses{def:gr_chart_transition', lem:gr_chartTransition'_ringIdentity, def:gr_away_pullback_iso, lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst, lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd, def:gr_chart_transition}
1399	  \leanok
1400	  Both fibre products are affine, so the identity may be checked after applying
1401	  \(\Spec\), as an identity of the underlying ring homomorphisms. Rewriting the
1402	  source projection \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) through the pullback identification
1403	  (\cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst}) and the target projection
1404	  \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) through \cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd} turns the claim
1405	  into the ring identity \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_ringIdentity}: the three
1406	  localised pieces of \(t'\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}) collapse to the
1407	  left inclusion followed by the plain transition \(t_{I,J}\)
1408	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}). Proved directly in Lean.
1409	\end{proof}
1410	
1411	\begin{lemma}
1412	
1413	\leanok
1414	[The triple-overlap cocycle coherence]
1415	  \label{lem:gr_chartTransition'_cocycle}
1416	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartTransition'_cocycle}
1417	  \uses{def:gr_chart_transition', lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id, def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}
1418	  The triple-overlap transitions \(t'\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}) satisfy
1419	  the cocycle coherence on the source triple overlap \(U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K\):
1420	  \[
1421	    t'_{I,J,K} \;\circ\; t'_{J,K,I} \;\circ\; t'_{K,I,J} \;=\; \mathrm{id}.
1422	  \]
1423	  This is the \(\mathrm{cocycle}\) field of the Grassmannian glue datum -- the
1424	  categorical realisation of the rotated ring identity \(\Phi = \id\)
1425	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id}).
1426	\end{lemma}
1427	\begin{proof}
1428	  \uses{def:gr_chart_transition', lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id, def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}
1429	  \leanok
1430	  Expand each factor \(t'\) into its constituents
1431	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}): a source pullback identification, the
1432	  \(\Spec\) of a localised triple-overlap transition \(\Theta\)
1433	  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}) pre-composed with the \(\Spec\) of an
1434	  order-swap (\cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}), and an inverse target pullback
1435	  identification. In the threefold composite the two internal pairs
1436	  \(\mathrm{ap}^{-1} \circ \mathrm{ap}\) of pullback identifications
1437	  (\cref{def:gr_away_pullback_iso}) cancel, adjacent factors sharing the same
1438	  identification. There remain the six \(\Spec\)-arrows of the three \(\Theta\)'s
1439	  and three order-swaps; these collapse into a single \(\Spec\) of their composite
1440	  ring endomorphism \(\Phi\). By \cref{lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id} one has
1441	  \(\Phi = \id\), so this middle \(\Spec\) is the identity, and the outer
1442	  conjugating pullback isomorphism cancels against its inverse, leaving the
1443	  identity of the source triple overlap. Proved directly in Lean.
1444	\end{proof}
1445	
1446	\begin{lemma}
1447	
1448	\leanok
1449	[Rotation lemma for the triple-overlap transitions]
1450	  \label{lem:gr_rotMid}
1451	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.rotMid}
1452	  \uses{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_cocycle_theta_jk, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv, lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_algebraMap, lem:gr_awayMulCommEquiv_comp_awayInclLeft}
1453	  Conjugating the rotated transition \(\Theta_{J,K,I}\) (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij})
